question:
One dimensional gas: The transfer matrix method can also be applied to a one dimensional gas of particles with short-range interactions.
(a) Show that for a potential with a hard core that screens the interactions from further neighbors, the Hamiltonian for N particles can be written as
$$ \mathcal{H} = \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{p_i^2}{2m} + \sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(x_i-x_{i-1}). $$
The (indistinguishable) particles are labeled with coordinates \{x_i\} such that
$$ 0 \le x_1 \le x_2 \le \dots \le x_N \le L, $$
where L is the length of the box confining the particles.
(b) Write the expression for the partition function Z(T,N,L). Change variables to \delta_1 = x_1, \delta_2 = x_2 - x_1, \dots, \delta_N = x_N - x_{N-1}, and carefully indicate the allowed ranges of integration and the constraints.
(c) Consider the Gibbs partition function obtained from the Laplace transformation
$$ Z(T,N,P) = \int_0^\infty dL \exp(-\beta P L) Z(T,N,L), $$
and by extremizing the integrand find the standard formula for P in the canonical ensemble.
(d) Change variables from L to \delta_{N+1} = L - \sum_{i=1}^N \delta_i, and find the expression for Z(T,N,P) as a product over one-dimensional integrals over each \delta_i.
(e) At a fixed pressure P, find expressions for the mean length L(T,N,P), and the density n = N/L(T,N,P) (involving ratios of integrals which should be easy to interpret).
(f) For a hard sphere gas, with minimum separation a between particles, calculate the equation of state P(T,n).
solution:
(a) Hard cores screen further-neighbor interactions, leaving only nearest-neighbor potentials. Including kinetic energies,
$$ \mathcal{H} = \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{p_i^2}{2m} + \sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(x_i-x_{i-1}), \quad 0 \le x_1 \le x_2 \le \cdots \le x_N \le L. $$

(b) The canonical partition function is
$$ Z(T,N,L) = \frac{1}{h^N} \int_0^L dx_1 \int_{x_1}^L dx_2 \cdots \int_{x_{N-1}}^L dx_N\, e^{-\beta \sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(x_i-x_{i-1})} \prod_{i=1}^N \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} dp_i\, e^{-\beta p_i^2/(2m)}. $$
After Gaussian momentum integrals with \lambda = h/\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T},
$$ Z(T,N,L) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N} \int_0^L dx_1 \int_{x_1}^L dx_2 \cdots \int_{x_{N-1}}^L dx_N\, e^{-\beta \sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(x_i-x_{i-1})}. $$
Change variables \delta_1=x_1, \delta_{i}=x_i-x_{i-1} (i\ge 2) so that x_N=\sum_{i=1}^N \delta_i. Then
$$ Z(T,N,L) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N} \int_0^L d\delta_1 \int_0^{L-\delta_1} d\delta_2 \cdots \int_0^{L-\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\delta_i} d\delta_N\, e^{-\beta\sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(\delta_i)}. $$
Equivalently,
$$ Z(T,N,L) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N} \int_0^\infty \prod_{i=1}^N d\delta_i\, e^{-\beta\sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(\delta_i)}\, \Theta\!\left(L-\sum_{i=1}^N\delta_i\right). $$

(c) The Gibbs partition function is
$$ Z(T,N,P) = \int_0^\infty dL\, e^{-\beta PL} Z(T,N,L). $$
Extremizing the integrand with respect to L gives the canonical pressure relation
$$ P = k_B T\, \left.\frac{\partial \ln Z}{\partial L}\right|_{T,N}, $$
consistent with F = -k_B T\ln Z and P = -\left.\partial F/\partial L\right|_{T,N}.

(d) Performing the Laplace transform using the step-function representation yields
$$ Z(T,N,P) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N (\beta P)^2} \left\{ \int_0^\infty d\delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]} \right\}^{N-1}. $$
An equivalent derivation introduces \delta_{N+1} = L - \sum_{i=1}^N \delta_i and integrates each \delta independently from 0 to \infty.

(e) The mean length is
$$ L(T,N,P) = -k_B T\, \frac{\partial}{\partial P} \ln Z(T,N,P)\bigg|_{T,N} = \frac{2}{\beta P} + (N-1) \frac{\int_0^\infty d\delta\, \delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]}}{\int_0^\infty d\delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]}}. $$
Thus
$$ n = \frac{N}{L(T,N,P)} = N \left\{ \frac{2k_B T}{P} + (N-1) \frac{\int_0^\infty d\delta\, \delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]}}{\int_0^\infty d\delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]}} \right\}^{-1}. $$
For an ideal gas (\mathcal{V}=0), L_{\text{i.g.}}(T,N,P) = (N+1)k_B T/P, and n_{\text{i.g.}} = \frac{N}{N+1}\, \frac{P}{k_B T}.

(f) Hard spheres: impose \delta_i \ge a for i=2,\dots,N, so
$$ Z(T,N,P) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N (\beta P)^2} \left[ \int_a^\infty d\delta\, e^{-\beta P\delta} \right]^{N-1} = \frac{1}{\lambda^N} \left( \frac{1}{\beta P} \right)^{N+1} e^{-\beta P a (N-1)}. $$
Then
$$ L = -k_B T \frac{\partial \ln Z}{\partial P}\bigg|_{T,N} = \frac{(N+1)k_B T}{P} + (N-1)a. $$
Rearranging,
$$ \beta P = \frac{N+1}{L-(N-1)a} = \frac{n+1/L}{1-(n-1/L)a} \approx n\,(1+na+n^2a^2+\cdots), $$
so the virial series is \beta P \approx n + a n^2 + a^2 n^3 + \cdots with B_\ell(T) = a^{\ell-1}.
final_answers: ["\\mathcal{H} = \\sum_{i=1}^N \\frac{p_i^2}{2m} + \\sum_{i=2}^N \\mathcal{V}(x_i-x_{i-1})", "Z(T,N,L) = \\frac{1}{\\lambda^N} \\int_0^L d\\delta_1\\!\\int_0^{L-\\delta_1}\\! d\\delta_2\\cdots\\int_0^{L-\\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\\delta_i} d\\delta_N\\, e^{-\\beta\\sum_{i=2}^N \\mathcal{V}(\\delta_i)}\\ (\\text{or with }\\Theta)", "P = k_B T\\, (\\partial \\ln Z/\\partial L)_{T,N}", "Z(T,N,P) = \\frac{1}{\\lambda^N (\\beta P)^2} \\Big\\{ \\int_0^\\infty d\\delta\\, e^{-\\beta[\\mathcal{V}(\\delta)+P\\delta]} \\Big\\}^{N-1}", "L = \\frac{2}{\\beta P} + (N-1) \\frac{\\int_0^\\infty d\\delta\\, \\delta\\, e^{-\\beta[\\mathcal{V}(\\delta)+P\\delta]}}{\\int_0^\\infty d\\delta\\, e^{-\\beta[\\mathcal{V}(\\delta)+P\\delta]}};\\ n = N/L", "\\beta P \\approx n + a n^2 + a^2 n^3 + \\cdots"]

question:
The Lorentz gas describes non-interacting particles colliding with a fixed set of scatterers. Consider a uniform two dimensional density $n_0$ of fixed impurities, which are hard circles of radius $a$.

(a) Show that the differential cross section of a hard circle scattering through an angle $\theta$ is $d\sigma = \tfrac{a}{2} \sin(\theta/2)\, d\theta$, and calculate the total cross section.

(b) Write down the Boltzmann equation for the one particle density $f(\vec{q}, \vec{p}, t)$ of the Lorentz gas (including only collisions with the fixed impurities). Ignore the electron spin.

(c) Find the eigenfunctions and eigenvalues of the collision operator.

(d) Using $\vec{F}\equiv -\partial U/\partial\vec{q}$, and
$$
 n(\vec{q},t) = \int d^{2}\vec{p} \, f(\vec{q},\vec{p},t), \qquad \langle g(\vec{q},t) \rangle = \frac{1}{n(\vec{q},t)} \int d^{2}\vec{p} \, f(\vec{q},\vec{p},t) \, g(\vec{q},t),
$$
show that for any function $\chi(|\vec{p}|)$,
$\displaystyle \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \big(n \langle \chi \rangle \big) + \frac{\partial}{\partial \vec{q}} \cdot \Big( n \big\langle (\vec{p}/m) \, \chi \big\rangle \Big) = \vec{F} \cdot \Big( n \big\langle \partial \chi/\partial \vec{p} \big\rangle \Big)$.

(e) Derive the conservation equation for local density $\rho\equiv m n(\vec q,t)$, in terms of the local velocity $\vec u \equiv \langle \vec p/m\rangle$.

(f) Since the magnitude of particle momentum is unchanged by impurity scattering, the Lorentz gas has an infinity of conserved quantities $|\vec p|^{m}$. This unrealistic feature is removed upon inclusion of particle-particle collisions. For the rest of this problem focus only on $p^2/(2m)$ as a conserved quantity. Derive the conservation equation for the energy density $\epsilon(\vec q,t) \equiv (\rho/2)\langle c^2\rangle$, where $\vec c \equiv \vec p/m - \vec u$, in terms of the energy flux $\vec h \equiv \rho \langle \vec c \, c^2\rangle/2$, and the pressure tensor $P_{\alpha\beta} \equiv \rho \langle c_\alpha c_\beta\rangle$.

(g) Starting with a one particle density reflecting local equilibrium,
$\displaystyle f^{0}(\vec p,\vec q,t)= \frac{n(\vec q,t)}{2\pi m k_B T(\vec q,t)} \exp\big[-p^2/(2 m k_B T(\vec q,t))\big]$, calculate $\vec u$, $\vec h$, and $P_{\alpha\beta}$. Hence obtain the zeroth order hydrodynamic equations.

(h) Show that in the single collision time approximation $C[f]=(f^0-f)/\tau$, the first order solution is
$$
 f^{1}(\vec p,\vec q,t)= f^{0}(\vec p,\vec q,t)\left[1-\tau\, \frac{\vec p}{m}\cdot\left( \nabla \ln\rho - \nabla \ln T + \frac{p^2}{2 m k_B T^2} \, \nabla T - \frac{\vec F}{k_B T}\right)\right].
$$

(i) Show that using the first order expression for $f$, we obtain $\rho \, \vec u = n\tau\, \big[ \vec F - k_B T \, \nabla \ln(\rho T) \big]$.

(j) From the above equation, calculate the velocity response function $\chi_{\alpha\beta} = \partial u_\alpha/\partial F_\beta$.

(k) Calculate $P_{\alpha\beta}$ and $\vec h$, and hence write down the first order hydrodynamic equations.
solution:
(a) With impact parameter $b=a\cos(\theta/2)$ for hard-disk scattering, the differential cross section in 2D is $d\sigma = 2|db| = a\sin(\theta/2)\, d\theta/\,1 = (a/2)\sin(\theta/2)\, d\theta$. The total cross section is $\sigma_{\rm tot}=\int_0^{\pi} d\theta\, a\sin(\theta/2) = 2a$.

(b) The Boltzmann equation with fixed scatterers of density $n_0$ is
$\partial_t f + (\vec p/m)\cdot\partial_{\vec q} f + \vec F\cdot\partial_{\vec p} f = (n_0 |\vec p|/m) \int d\theta\, (d\sigma/d\theta) [f(\vec p') - f(\vec p)] \equiv C[f(\vec p)]$.

(c) [No solution provided in the source.]

(d) Multiply the Boltzmann equation by $\chi(|\vec p|)$ and integrate over $\vec p$; streaming terms give the divergence term, force term yields $\vec F\cdot n\langle \partial \chi/\partial\vec p\rangle$, and the collision integral vanishes for any function of $|\vec p|$ (isotropic in angle), giving $\partial_t(n\langle\chi\rangle)+\partial_{\vec q}\cdot\big(n\langle (\vec p/m)\chi\rangle\big)=\vec F\cdot n\langle \partial\chi/\partial\vec p\rangle$.

(e) For $\chi=1$, $\partial_t n + \partial_{\vec q}\cdot(n\langle \vec p/m\rangle)=0$. With $\rho=mn$ and $\vec u=\langle \vec p/m\rangle$, $\partial_t\rho + \partial_{\vec q}\cdot(\rho\vec u)=0$.

(f) For $\chi=p^2/(2m)$, one finds $\partial_t[\tfrac{\rho}{2} u^2 + \epsilon] + \partial_{\vec q}\cdot\big[ \vec u(\tfrac{\rho}{2}u^2+\epsilon) + \vec h\big] + \partial_{q_\alpha}(u_\beta P_{\alpha\beta}) = (\rho/m)\, \vec F\cdot\vec u$, with $\epsilon=\rho\langle c^2\rangle/2$, $\vec h=\rho\langle \vec c c^2\rangle/2$, $P_{\alpha\beta}=\rho\langle c_\alpha c_\beta\rangle$.

(g) For local Maxwellian $f^0$, $\vec u=\langle \vec p/m\rangle_0=0$, $\vec h=\rho\langle \vec c c^2\rangle_0/2=0$ (odd moments vanish), and $P_{\alpha\beta}=n k_B T\, \delta_{\alpha\beta}$. Zeroth-order hydrodynamics then gives $\partial_t\rho=0$ and $\partial_t\epsilon=0$ for stationary $n(\vec q)$ and $T(\vec q)$.

(h) With $C[f]=(f^0-f)/\tau$ and $f=f^0(1+g)$, the first-order correction is $g= -\tau\, [\partial_t\ln f^0 + (\vec p/m)\cdot\nabla\ln f^0 + \vec F\cdot\partial_{\vec p}\ln f^0]$. Using $\ln f^0 = -p^2/(2mk_BT) + \ln n - \ln T - \ln(2\pi m k_B)$ (time-independent $n,T$) yields the stated $f^1$.

(i) Using $f\approx f^0(1+g)$, $\rho\, u_\alpha = m \int d^2p\, (p_\alpha/m) f \;=\; n\tau\, [F_\alpha - k_B T\, \partial_{q_\alpha}\ln(\rho T)]$.

(j) $\chi_{\alpha\beta}=\partial u_\alpha/\partial F_\beta = (n\tau/\rho)\, \delta_{\alpha\beta}$.

(k) To first order, $P_{\alpha\beta}=n k_B T\, \delta_{\alpha\beta}$ and $\delta^1 P_{\alpha\beta}=0$. The heat flux evaluates to $h_\alpha = -12\, n k_B^2 T\, \tau\, \partial_{q_\alpha} T$. Substituting into (f) gives the first-order energy equation: $\partial_t[\tfrac{\rho}{2}u^2+\epsilon] + \partial_{\vec q}\cdot[\vec u(\tfrac{\rho}{2}u^2+\epsilon) - 11 n k_B^2 T\,\tau\, \nabla T] + \partial_{\vec q}\cdot(\vec u\, n k_B T) = (\rho/m)\, \vec F\cdot\vec u$.
final_answers: ["$d\\sigma = (a/2)\\sin(\\theta/2)\\, d\\theta$, $\\;\\sigma_{\\rm tot}=2a$", "$\\partial_t f + (\\vec p/m)\\cdot\\partial_{\\vec q} f + \\vec F\\cdot\\partial_{\\vec p} f = (n_0 |\\vec p|/m) \\int d\\theta\\, (d\\sigma/d\\theta) [f(\\vec p') - f(\\vec p)]$", null, "$\\partial_t(n\\langle\\chi\\rangle)+\\partial_{\\vec q}\\cdot\\big(n\\langle (\\vec p/m)\\chi\\rangle\\big)=\\vec F\\cdot n\\langle \\partial\\chi/\\partial\\vec p\\rangle$", "$\\partial_t\\rho + \\nabla\\cdot(\\rho\\vec u)=0$", "$\\partial_t[\\tfrac{\\rho}{2} u^2 + \\epsilon] + \\nabla\\cdot[ \\vec u(\\tfrac{\\rho}{2}u^2+\\epsilon) + \\vec h] + \\partial_{q_\\alpha}(u_\\beta P_{\\alpha\\beta}) = (\\rho/m)\\, \\vec F\\cdot\\vec u$", "$\\vec u=0,\\; \\vec h=0,\\; P_{\\alpha\\beta}=n k_B T\\, \\delta_{\\alpha\\beta}$; zeroth-order hydrodynamics stationary", "$f^{1}= f^{0}\\Big[1-\\tau\\, \\dfrac{\\vec p}{m}\\cdot\\Big( \\nabla \\ln\\rho - \\nabla \\ln T + \\dfrac{p^2}{2 m k_B T^2} \\, \\nabla T - \\dfrac{\\vec F}{k_B T}\\Big)\\Big]$", "$\\rho\\, \\vec u = n\\tau\\, \\big[ \\vec F - k_B T \\, \\nabla \\ln(\\rho T) \\big]$", "$\\chi_{\\alpha\\beta}= (n\\tau/\\rho)\\, \\delta_{\\alpha\\beta}$", "$P_{\\alpha\\beta}=n k_B T\\,\\delta_{\\alpha\\beta}$, $\\; \\vec h = -12\\, n k_B^2 T\\, \\tau\\, \\nabla T$"]

question:
3. Potts model II: An alternative expansion is obtained by starting with
$$
\exp[K\,\delta(s_i,s_j)] = 1 + v(K)\,\delta(s_i,s_j),\qquad v(K)=\mathrm{e}^K-1.
$$
In this case, the sum over spins does not remove any graphs, and all choices of distributing bonds at random on the lattice are acceptable.

(a) Including a magnetic field \(h \sum_i \delta_{s_i,1}\), show that the partition function takes the form
$$
Z(q,K,h) = \sum_{\text{all graphs}} \prod_{\text{clusters }c \text{ in graph}} \big[ v^{n_b^c} \times (q-1 + \mathrm{e}^{h n_s^c})\big],
$$
where \(n_b^c\) and \(n_s^c\) are the numbers of bonds and sites in cluster c. This is the random cluster expansion.

(b) Show that the limit \(q\to 1\) describes a percolation problem, in which bonds are randomly distributed on the lattice with probability \(p = v/(v+1)\). What is the percolation threshold on the square lattice?

(c) Show that in the limit \(q\to 0\), only a single connected cluster contributes to leading order. The enumeration of all such clusters is known as listing branched lattice animals.
solution:
(a) With field along state 1,
$$
Z = \sum_{\{s_i\}} \prod_{\langle ij \rangle} [1+v\,\delta(s_i,s_j)] \prod_i \mathrm{e}^{h\,\delta_{s_i,1}}.
$$
Expanding in \(v\), each term corresponds to a graph of included bonds; each included bond carries a factor v and forces equality of spins at its endpoints. Bonds partition the lattice into clusters; within a cluster all spins are equal. Summing over spins gives a factor \((q-1)+\mathrm{e}^{h n_s^c}\) for cluster c (with \(n_s^c\) its number of sites). Therefore
$$
Z(q,v,h) = \sum_{\text{all graphs}} \prod_{\text{clusters }c} v^{n_b^c}\,\big(q-1+\mathrm{e}^{h n_s^c}\big),
$$
with \(n_b^c\) the number of bonds in c.

(b) In bond percolation, bonds are occupied independently with probability p; a configuration has weight
$$
W(\text{graph}) = (1-p)^{N_B} \prod_{\text{clusters }c} \Big(\frac{p}{1-p}\Big)^{n_b^c}.
$$
This matches the Potts random-cluster weights at \(q=1\) (and \(h=0\)) if
$$
p = \frac{v}{1+v},
$$
up to an overall analytic factor \((1+v)^{N_B}\). The percolation threshold on the square lattice follows from Potts duality (problem 2): \(v^*=1\Rightarrow p_c = v^*/(1+v^*) = 1/2\).

(c) For \(q\to 0\), set \(v = x q^a\). Then
$$
Z(q, v=x q^a, h=0) = \sum_{\text{graphs}} x^{N_b} q^{N_c + a N_b},
$$
where \(N_b\) and \(N_c\) are the total numbers of bonds and clusters. As \(q\to 0\), graphs minimizing \(N_c + a N_b\) dominate. For \(0<a<1\), these are spanning trees (single cluster, no loops). For \(a=0\), all single-cluster graphs contribute with the same power of q, leading to branched lattice animals.
final_answers: ["Z(q,v,h) = \\sum_{\\text{graphs}} \\prod_{c} v^{n_b^c}\\,\\big(q-1+\\mathrm{e}^{h n_s^c}\\big)", "p = v/(1+v),\\; p_c=1/2", null]

question:
Zeroth-order hydrodynamics: The hydrodynamic equations resulting from conservation laws in a uniform box are
$$
\begin{cases}
\partial_t n + \partial_\alpha (n u_\alpha) = 0 \\
\partial_t u_\alpha + u_\beta \, \partial_\beta u_\alpha = -\frac{1}{mn} \, \partial_\beta P_{\alpha \beta} \\
\partial_t \varepsilon + u_\alpha \, \partial_\alpha \varepsilon = -\frac{1}{n} \, \partial_\alpha h_\alpha - \frac{1}{n} \, P_{\alpha \beta} \, u_{\alpha \beta}
\end{cases}
$$
where $\vec u = \langle \vec p/m\rangle$, $u_{\alpha\beta}=(\partial_\alpha u_\beta+\partial_\beta u_\alpha)/2$, and $\varepsilon=\langle m c^2/2\rangle$ with $\vec c=\vec p/m - \vec u$.

(a) For the zeroth order density
$\displaystyle f_1^0(\vec p, \vec q, t) = \frac{n(\vec q, t)}{(2 \pi m k_B T(\vec q, t))^{3/2}} \exp \Big[ -\frac{(\vec p - m \vec u(\vec q, t))^2}{2 m k_B T(\vec q, t)} \Big]$,
calculate the pressure tensor $P_{\alpha\beta}^0 = mn \langle c_\alpha c_\beta \rangle^0$ and the heat flux $h_\alpha^0 = nm \langle c_\alpha c^2/2 \rangle^0$.

(b) Obtain the zeroth order hydrodynamic equations governing the evolution of $n(\vec q,t)$, $\vec u(\vec q,t)$, and $T(\vec q,t)$.

(c) Show that the above equations imply $D_t \ln (n T^{-3/2}) = 0$, where $D_t = \partial_t + u_\beta \partial_\beta$.

(d) Write down the expression for $H^0(t) = \int d^3\vec q\, d^3\vec p\, f_1^0\, \ln f_1^0$ after integrating over $\vec p$.

(e) Using the hydrodynamic equations in (b) calculate $dH^0/dt$.

(f) Discuss the implications of the result in (e) for the approach to equilibrium.
solution:
(a) The distribution of $\vec c$ is Gaussian $\propto e^{-m c^2/(2k_B T)}$. Thus $\langle c_\alpha c_\beta\rangle^0 = (k_B T/m)\, \delta_{\alpha\beta}$, so $P_{\alpha\beta}^0 = n k_B T\, \delta_{\alpha\beta}$. Odd moments vanish, hence $h_\alpha^0=0$, and $\varepsilon=\tfrac{3}{2}k_B T$.

(b) Substituting $P_{\alpha\beta}^0=n k_B T\, \delta_{\alpha\beta}$, $\varepsilon=\tfrac{3}{2}k_B T$, and $h_\alpha^0=0$ into the hydrodynamic equations gives
$\begin{cases}
\partial_t n + u_\alpha \partial_\alpha n = -n\, \partial_\alpha u_\alpha, \\
\partial_t u_\alpha + u_\beta \partial_\beta u_\alpha = -\dfrac{k_B}{m n}\, \partial_\alpha (nT), \\
\tfrac{3}{2}(\partial_t T + u_\alpha \partial_\alpha T) = -T\, \partial_\alpha u_\alpha.
\end{cases}$

(c) Using $D_t=\partial_t + u_\beta\partial_\beta$, the first and third equations become $D_t\ln n = -\partial_\alpha u_\alpha$ and $(3/2) D_t\ln T = -\partial_\alpha u_\alpha$. Eliminating $\partial_\alpha u_\alpha$ gives $D_t\ln(n T^{-3/2})=0$.

(d) With $\ln f_1^0 = \ln n - (3/2)\ln(2\pi m k_B T) - (\vec p - m\vec u)^2/(2 m k_B T)$, Gaussian integration over $\vec p$ yields
$\displaystyle H^0(t) = \int d^3\vec q\; n\,\Big[\ln(n T^{-3/2}) - \tfrac{3}{2}\ln(2\pi m k_B) - \tfrac{3}{2}\Big].$

(e) Differentiating and using (b) and (c): $\dfrac{dH^0}{dt} = -\int d^3\vec q\, \partial_\alpha\big[ n u_\alpha\, \ln(n T^{-3/2})\big]=0$ (surface term vanishes).

(f) Since $-H^0$ is proportional to the entropy for the local Maxwellian, (e) shows that along zeroth-order hydrodynamic evolution the entropy does not change; first-order (dissipative) corrections are needed to describe entropy increase towards equilibrium.
final_answers: ["$P_{\\alpha\\beta}^0 = n k_B T\\, \\delta_{\\alpha\\beta}$; $\\; h_\\alpha^0=0$", "$\\begin{cases}\\partial_t n + u_\\alpha\\partial_\\alpha n = -n\\,\\partial_\\alpha u_\\alpha \\\\ \\partial_t u_\\alpha + u_\\beta\\partial_\\beta u_\\alpha = -(k_B/(mn))\\, \\partial_\\alpha(nT) \\\\ \\tfrac{3}{2}(\\partial_t T + u_\\alpha\\partial_\\alpha T) = -T\\,\\partial_\\alpha u_\\alpha \\end{cases}$", "$D_t\\ln(n T^{-3/2})=0$", "$H^0= \\int d^3\\vec q\\; n\\,\\big[\\ln(n T^{-3/2}) - \\tfrac{3}{2}\\ln(2\\pi m k_B) - \\tfrac{3}{2}\\big]$", "$dH^0/dt=0$", null]

question:
Two component plasma: Consider a neutral mixture of N ions of charge $+e$ and mass $m_+$, and N electrons of charge $-e$ and mass $m_-$, in a volume $V = N/n_0$.

(a) Show that the Vlasov equations for this two component system are
$$
\begin{cases}
\left[\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\vec{p}}{m_+} \cdot \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \vec{q}} + e \dfrac{\partial \Phi_{\text{eff}}}{\partial \vec{q}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \vec{p}}\right] f_{+}(\vec{p}, \vec{q}, t) = 0 \\
\left[\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\vec{p}}{m_-} \cdot \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \vec{q}} - e \dfrac{\partial \Phi_{\text{eff}}}{\partial \vec{q}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \vec{p}}\right] f_{-}(\vec{p}, \vec{q}, t) = 0
\end{cases}
$$
where the effective Coulomb potential is
$\displaystyle \Phi_{\text{eff}}(\vec{q},t) = \Phi_{\text{ext}}(\vec{q}) + e \int d\mathbf{x}' \, C(\vec{q} - \vec{q}') \big[f_+(\mathbf{x}',t) - f_-(\mathbf{x}',t)\big]$, and $C$ satisfies $\nabla^2 C = 4\pi \delta^3(\vec q)$.

(b) Assume that the one particle densities have the stationary forms $f_{\pm} = g_{\pm}(\vec{p}) n_{\pm}(\vec{q})$. Show that the effective potential satisfies the equation
$\nabla^{2} \Phi_{\text{eff}} = 4 \pi \rho_{\text{ext}} + 4 \pi e \big( n_{+}(\vec{q}) - n_{-}(\vec{q}) \big)$, where $\rho_{\text{ext}}$ is the external charge density.

(c) Further assuming that the densities relax to the equilibrium Boltzmann weights $n_{\pm}(\vec{q}) = n_0 \exp[ \pm \beta e \, \Phi_{\mathrm{eff}}(\vec{q}) ]$, derive the Debye equation $\nabla^{2} \Phi_{\text{eff}} = 4\pi \rho_{\text{ext}} + \Phi_{\text{eff}}/\lambda^{2}$ by linearizing the exponentials, and give the Debye screening length $\lambda$.

(d) Show that the Debye equation has the general solution $\Phi_{\text{eff}}(\vec{q}) = \int d^{3}\vec{q}'\, G(\vec{q} - \vec{q}') \, \rho_{\text{ext}}(\vec{q}')$ where $G(\vec{q}) = e^{-|
\vec{q}|/\lambda}/|
\vec{q}|$ is the screened Coulomb potential.

(e) Give the condition for the self-consistency of the Vlasov approximation, and interpret it in terms of the inter-particle spacing.

(f) Show that the characteristic relaxation time ($\tau \approx \lambda/c$) is temperature independent. What property of the plasma is it related to?

(g) Write down the most general form for the equilibrium distribution functions for particles confined to a circularly symmetric potential.

(h) How is the result in part (g) modified by including scattering from magnetic and non-magnetic impurities?

(i) Do conservation of spin and angular momentum lead to new hydrodynamic equations?
solution:
(a) For ions/electrons with charges $\pm e$, the mean-field Hamiltonian leads to Vlasov streaming with Lorentz-like force from $\Phi_{\rm eff}$. Substituting $\partial_{\vec q}\mathcal H = \pm e\,\partial_{\vec q} \Phi_{\rm eff}$ yields
$\big[\partial_t + (\vec p/m_+)\cdot\partial_{\vec q} + e\,\partial_{\vec q} \Phi_{\rm eff}\cdot\partial_{\vec p}\big] f_+ = 0$, and $\big[\partial_t + (\vec p/m_-)\cdot\partial_{\vec q} - e\,\partial_{\vec q} \Phi_{\rm eff}\cdot\partial_{\vec p}\big] f_- = 0$, with
$\Phi_{\rm eff}(\vec q,t)=\Phi_{\rm ext}(\vec q)+ e\int d\mathbf x'\, C(\vec q-\vec q')\,[f_+(\mathbf x',t)-f_-(\mathbf x',t)]$ and $\nabla^2 C=4\pi\delta^3$.

(b) With $f_\pm(\vec p,\vec q)=g_\pm(\vec p) n_\pm(\vec q)$ and $\int d^3p\, g_\pm=1$, the effective potential becomes $\Phi_{\rm eff}(\vec q) = \Phi_{\rm ext}(\vec q) + e \int d^3q'\, C(\vec q-\vec q') [n_+(\vec q')-n_-(\vec q')]$. Applying $\nabla^2$ and using $\nabla^2 C=4\pi \delta^3$ gives $\nabla^2\Phi_{\rm eff} = 4\pi \rho_{\rm ext} + 4\pi e (n_+ - n_-)$.

(c) Linearizing $n_\pm \approx n_0[1 \pm \beta e \Phi_{\rm eff}]$ yields $n_+-n_- \approx 2 n_0 \beta e \Phi_{\rm eff}$. Then $\nabla^2 \Phi_{\rm eff} = 4\pi \rho_{\rm ext} + (1/\lambda^2)\,\Phi_{\rm eff}$ with $\lambda^2 = k_B T/(8\pi n_0 e^2)$.

(d) The Green's function of $\nabla^2 - 1/\lambda^2$ in 3D is $G(\vec r) = e^{-r/\lambda}/r$. Thus $\Phi_{\rm eff}(\vec q)=\int d^3q'\, G(\vec q-\vec q')\,\rho_{\rm ext}(\vec q')$ solves the Debye equation.

(e) Self-consistency requires many particles within a Debye sphere: $n_0 \lambda^3 \gg 1$, equivalently $(k_B T)^{3/2}/(n_0^{1/2} e^3) \gg 1$, or $e^2/\ell \ll k_B T$ with interparticle spacing $\ell \sim n_0^{-1/3}$.

(f) $\tau \sim \lambda/c \sim \sqrt{\tfrac{k_B T}{n_0 e^2}}\, \sqrt{\tfrac{m}{k_B T}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{m}{n_0 e^2}} = 1/\omega_p$, independent of $T$, where $\omega_p$ is the plasma frequency.

(g) With conserved one-body invariants $\mathcal H_\alpha$ and $L_z$ in a circular potential, equilibrium has the form $f_\alpha = A_\alpha \exp\big[-\beta \mathcal H_\alpha - \gamma L_z\big]$.

(h) Impurity scattering breaks conservation of $\vec p$ and hence $L_z$; the $\gamma L_z$ term is removed. Magnetic impurities mix spins requiring $A_\uparrow = A_\downarrow$; non-magnetic impurities do not enforce this equality.

(i) Angular momentum conservation in 2D is not independent of momentum conservation and adds no new hydrodynamic equation, while spin conservation adds an additional hydrodynamic equation for magnetization $\propto (n_\uparrow - n_\downarrow)$.
final_answers: ["Two Vlasov equations as given with $\\Phi_{\\rm eff}(\\vec q,t)=\\Phi_{\\rm ext}(\\vec q)+ e \\int d\\mathbf x'\\, C(\\vec q-\\vec q') [f_+ - f_-]$", "$\\nabla^2\\Phi_{\\rm eff} = 4\\pi \\rho_{\\rm ext} + 4\\pi e (n_+ - n_-)$", "Debye: $\\nabla^2\\Phi_{\\rm eff} = 4\\pi \\rho_{\\rm ext} + \\Phi_{\\rm eff}/\\lambda^2$ with $\\lambda^2=\\dfrac{k_B T}{8\\pi n_0 e^2}$", "$\\Phi_{\\rm eff}(\\vec q)=\\int d^3q'\\, \\dfrac{e^{-|\n\\vec q-\\vec q'|/\\lambda}}{|\n\\vec q-\\vec q'|}\\,\\rho_{\\rm ext}(\\vec q')$", "Self-consistency: $n_0\\lambda^3\\gg 1$ (or $e^2/\\ell \\ll k_B T$)", "$\\tau\\sim \\lambda/c \\sim 1/\\omega_p$ (temperature independent)", "$f_\\alpha = A_\\alpha\\, e^{-\\beta \\mathcal H_\\alpha - \\gamma L_z}$", "Remove $\\gamma L_z$; magnetic impurities enforce $A_\\uparrow=A_\\downarrow$", "Spin adds a new equation; angular momentum does not"]

question:
(a) A single particle confined to move solely in the  $x-y$  plane is described by the single-particle wavefunction

(470)
$$
\Psi_m(z) = z^m \exp\left(-\frac{|z|^2}{4\ell^2}\right),
$$

Show that this wavefunction is an eigenstate of  $L_z$ .

(b) Now consider the generalization

(471)
$$
\Psi_m(z_1,...,z_N) = \prod_{j < k} (z_j - z_k)^m \exp\left(-\sum_{j=1}^N \frac{|z_j|^2}{4\ell}\right)
$$

to $N$ particles confined to move in the $x$–$y$ plane, where  $z_j = x_j + i y_j$ . For what values of $m$ is this wavefunction antisymmetric with respect to particle interchange?

(c) Show that this wavefunction is an eigenstate of the total  $L_z$  operator with eigenvalue

$$
(472)\qquad \qquad M = \frac{N(N-1)m}{2}.
$$

For $N$ particles, the total  $L_z$  operator is simply the sum of the  $L_z$  for each particle.
solution:
In the holomorphic representation,

(473)

$$
L_3 = -i\partial_{\theta}
$$

$$
= -i(\partial_{\theta}z)\partial_z - i(\partial_{\theta}\bar{z})\partial_{\bar{z}}
$$

$$
= z\partial_z - \bar{z}\partial_{\bar{z}}
$$

so that

(474)

$$
L_3 \Psi_m = (z \partial_z - \bar{z} \partial_{\bar{z}}) \left( z^m \exp\left( -\frac{\bar{z}z}{4l^2} \right) \right)
$$

$$
= z m z^{m-1} \exp\left( -\frac{\bar{z}z}{4 l^2} \right) - \left( \frac{\bar{z}z}{4 l^2} \right) z^m \exp\left( -\frac{\bar{z}z}{4 l^2} \right) + \left( \frac{\bar{z}}{4 l^2} \right) z^m \exp\left( -\frac{\bar{z}z}{4 l^2} \right)
$$

$$
= m z^m \exp\left( -\frac{\bar{z}z}{4 l^2} \right)
$$

$$
= m \Psi_m.
$$

Suppose particles $a$ and $b$ switch. Only the discriminant  $\prod_{j < k} (z_j - z_k)^m$  contributes a sign. Consider all $c$ such that  $a < c < b$ . Then the terms  $(z_a - z_c)^m$  and  $(z_c - z_b)^m$  are switched, contributing a total sign  $(-1)^{2m} = 1$ . The other contributing term is  $(z_a - z_b)^m$ , which yields  $(-1)^m$  when switched. Thus the wavefunction is antisymmetric iff  $m$  is odd. Now  $L_3 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (z_i \partial_{z_i} - \bar{z}_i \partial_{\bar{z}_i})$ . Then

$$
L_3\Psi_m = \sum_{i=1}^N (z_i \partial_{z_i} - \bar{z}_i \partial_{\bar{z}_i}) \prod_{1 \le j < k \le N} (z_j - z_k)^m \exp\left(-\sum_{j=1}^N \frac{\bar{z}_j z_j}{4l^2}\right)
$$

$$
= \exp\left(-\sum_{j=1}^N \frac{\bar{z}_j z_j}{4l^2}\right) \sum_{i=1}^N z_i \partial_{z_i} \prod_{1 \le j < k \le N} (z_j - z_k)^m
$$

$$
= \exp\left(-\sum_{j=1}^N \frac{\bar{z}_j z_j}{4l^2}\right) \left(\prod_{1 \le j < k \le N} (z_j - z_k)^m\right) \sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{1 \le j < k \le N} \frac{z_i(\delta_{ij} - \delta_{ik})m(z_j - z_k)^{m-1}}{(z_j - z_k)^m}
$$

$$
= m\Psi_m \sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{1 \le j < k \le N} \frac{z_i(\delta_{ij} - \delta_{ik})}{z_j - z_k}
$$

$$
= m\Psi_m \sum_{1 \le j < k \le N} 1
$$

$$
= \frac{N(N-1)m}{2}\Psi_m
$$

question:
4. Surfactant condensation: \(N\) surfactant molecules are added to the surface of water over an area \(A\). They are subject to a Hamiltonian
\[ \mathcal{H} = \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{\vec p_i^{\,2}}{2m} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} V(\vec r_i - \vec r_j), \]
where \(\vec r_i\) and \(\vec p_i\) are two-dimensional vectors indicating the position and momentum of particle \(i\). (The inter-particle potential \(V(r)\) is infinite for separations \(|\vec r|<a\), and attractive for \(|\vec r|>a\) such that \(\int_a^\infty 2\pi r\,dr\,V(r) = -u_0\).)
(a) Write down the expression for the partition function \(Z(N,T,A)\) in terms of integrals over \(\vec r_i\) and \(\vec p_i\), and perform the integrals over the momenta.
(b) Estimate the total non-excluded area available in the positional phase space of the system of \(N\) particles.
(c) Estimate the total potential energy of the system, assuming a uniform density \(n=N/A\). Using this potential energy for all configurations allowed in the previous part, write down an approximation for \(Z\).
(d) The surface tension of water without surfactants is \(\sigma_0\), approximately independent of temperature. Calculate the surface tension \(\sigma(n,T)\) in the presence of surfactants.
(e) Show that below a certain temperature, \(T_c\), the expression for \(\sigma\) is manifestly incorrect. What happens at low temperatures?
(f) Compute the heat capacities, \(C_A\) and write down an expression for \(C_\sigma\) (without explicit evaluation), due to the surfactants.
solution:
(a) The canonical partition function is
\[ Z(N,T,A) = \frac{1}{N!h^{2N}} \int \prod_{i=1}^N d^2 p_i\, d^2 q_i\, \exp\Big[ -\beta \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{p_i^2}{2m} - \beta \sum_{i<j} V(\vec q_i - \vec q_j) \Big]. \]
Integrating over 2D Gaussian momenta gives \(\lambda = h/\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}\) and
\[ Z(N,T,A) = \frac{1}{N!\,\lambda^{2N}} \int \prod_{i=1}^N d^2 q_i\, \exp\Big[ -\beta \sum_{i<j} V(\vec q_i - \vec q_j) \Big]. \]

(b) In the dilute limit with excluded area \(\Omega=\pi a^2\), adding particles sequentially gives an accessible area factor approximately
\[ A(A-\Omega)(A-2\Omega)\cdots (A-(N-1)\Omega) \approx (A - N\Omega/2)^N, \]
neglecting \(\Omega^2\) corrections.

(c) For uniform density \(n=N/A\), the mean attractive energy is
\[ \bar U \approx \frac{n^2}{2} A \int d^2 r\, V_{\text{attr}}(r) = -\frac{N^2}{2A} u_0. \]
Using this energy for all allowed configurations of (b) yields
\[ Z(N,T,A) \approx \frac{1}{N!\,\lambda^{2N}}\, (A - N\Omega/2)^N\, \exp\Big[ \frac{\beta u_0 N^2}{2A} \Big]. \]

(d) The free energy is \(F=-k_B T\ln Z\). The surfactant contribution to the surface tension is
\[ \sigma_s = \left.\frac{\partial F}{\partial A}\right|_{T,N} = -k_B T\,\left.\frac{\partial \ln Z}{\partial A}\right|_{T,N} = -\frac{N k_B T}{A - N\Omega/2} + \frac{u_0 N^2}{2 A^2}. \]
Adding the bare contribution \(\sigma_0\) gives
\[ \sigma(n,T) = \sigma_0 - \frac{N k_B T}{A - N\Omega/2} + \frac{u_0 N^2}{2 A^2}. \]

(e) Mechanical stability requires \(\partial \sigma/\partial A > 0\). Using \(\sigma_s\) above,
\[ \frac{\partial \sigma_s}{\partial A} = \frac{N k_B T}{(A - N\Omega/2)^2} - \frac{u_0 N^2}{A^3}, \quad \frac{\partial^2 \sigma_s}{\partial A^2} = -\frac{2 N k_B T}{(A - N\Omega/2)^3} + \frac{3 u_0 N^2}{A^4}. \]
These vanish simultaneously at
\[ A_c = \frac{3 N \Omega}{2}, \qquad T_c = \frac{8 u_0}{27 k_B \Omega}. \]
For \(T<T_c\), the mean-field isotherms become non-monotone, signaling phase separation of surfactants into coexisting high-density (liquid-like) and low-density (gas-like) phases on the surface (condensation).

(f) The surfactant energy is
\[ E_s = -\left.\frac{\partial \ln Z}{\partial \beta}\right|_{A,N} = N k_B T - \frac{u_0 N^2}{2A}. \]
Hence
\[ C_A = \left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right|_A = N k_B, \qquad C_\sigma = \left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right|_\sigma - \sigma\,\left.\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right|_\sigma. \]
final_answers: ["Z(N,T,A)=\\dfrac{1}{N!\\,\\lambda^{2N}} \\int \\prod_{i=1}^N d^2 q_i\\, \\exp\\Big[ -\\beta \\sum_{i<j} V(\\vec q_i - \\vec q_j) \\Big]", "\\text{Accessible area factor }\\approx (A - N\\Omega/2)^N", "\\bar U = -\\dfrac{N^2}{2A} u_0,\\ \\ Z \\approx \\dfrac{1}{N!\\,\\lambda^{2N}} (A - N\\Omega/2)^N \\exp\\Big[ \\dfrac{\\beta u_0 N^2}{2A} \\Big]", "\\sigma(n,T)=\\sigma_0 - \\dfrac{N k_B T}{A - N\\Omega/2} + \\dfrac{u_0 N^2}{2 A^2}", "A_c=\\dfrac{3 N \\Omega}{2},\\ \\ T_c=\\dfrac{8 u_0}{27 k_B \\Omega};\\ \\text{for }T<T_c\\text{ the system phase-separates}", "C_A=N k_B,\\ \\ C_\\sigma=\\left.\\dfrac{\\partial E}{\\partial T}\\right|_\\sigma - \\sigma\\,\\left.\\dfrac{\\partial A}{\\partial T}\\right|_\\sigma"]

question:
4. Equations of State: The equation of state constrains the form of internal energy as in the following examples.

(a) Starting from $dE = T dS - P dV$, show that the equation of state $PV = N k_{B} T$ implies that $E$ can only depend on $T$.

(b) What is the most general equation of state consistent with an internal energy that depends only on temperature?

(c) Show that for a van der Waals gas $C_V$ is a function of temperature alone.
solution:
(a) Take $T$ and $V$ as independent, with $E=E(T,V)$ and $S=S(T,V)$. Then
$$
dE = T\,dS - P\,dV = T\left.\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right|_V dT + \Big(T\left.\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right|_T - P\Big) dV.
$$
Using the Maxwell relation $\left.\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right|_T = \left.\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right|_V$ gives
$$
\left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial V}\right|_T = T\left.\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right|_V - P.
$$
For an ideal gas $P=Nk_B T/V$, so $T(\partial P/\partial T)_V = P$ and hence $(\partial E/\partial V)_T=0$. Thus $E=E(T)$.

(b) If $E=E(T)$, then $(\partial E/\partial V)_T=0$ implies $T(\partial P/\partial T)_V = P$, whose solution is $P = f(V)\,T$ with arbitrary $f(V)$.

(c) For the van der Waals gas
$$
P = \frac{N k_B T}{V - N b} + a\left(\frac{N}{V}\right)^2.
$$
Then
$$
\frac{\partial C_V}{\partial V}\Big|_T = \frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial V\,\partial T} = \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\Big(T\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\Big|_V - P\Big) = T\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial T^2}\Big|_V = 0,
$$
so $C_V$ depends only on $T$.

question:
Two dimensional electron gas in a magnetic field: Consider a classical gas of electrons confined to two dimensions in a uniform magnetic field $\vec B$.

(a) For non-interacting electrons with Hamiltonian $\mathcal H = \sum_i \big[(\vec p_i - e\vec A)^2/(2m) \pm \mu_B |\vec B|\big]$ with $\vec A= (\vec B\times \vec q)/2$, obtain the classical equations of motion and show they describe cyclotron orbits orthogonal to $\vec B$.

(b) Heuristically write the Boltzmann equations for the densities $f_{\uparrow}(\mathbf p,\mathbf q,t)$ and $f_{\downarrow}(\mathbf p,\mathbf q,t)$ of electrons with up and down spins, in terms of the two cross-sections $\sigma\equiv\sigma_{\uparrow\uparrow}=\sigma_{\downarrow\downarrow}$, and $\sigma_\times\equiv\sigma_{\uparrow\downarrow}$ for spin-conserving collisions.

(c) Show that $dH/dt\le 0$, where $H=H_\uparrow+H_\downarrow$ is the sum of the corresponding H functions.

(d) Show that $dH/dt=0$ for any $\ln f$ which is, at each location, a linear combination of quantities conserved in the collisions.

(e) Show that the streaming terms in the Boltzmann equation are zero for any function that depends only on the quantities conserved by the one-body Hamiltonians.

(f) Show that angular momentum $\vec L=\vec q\times \vec p$ is conserved during, and away from, collisions.

(g) Write down the most general form for the equilibrium distribution functions for particles confined to a circularly symmetric potential.

(h) How is the result in part (g) modified by including scattering from magnetic and non-magnetic impurities?

(i) Do conservation of spin and angular momentum lead to new hydrodynamic equations?
solution:
(a) With $\vec A=(\vec B\times\vec q)/2$, Hamilton's equations yield $\dot{\vec q}=\partial \mathcal H/\partial \vec p = (\vec p - e\vec A)/m$, and $\dot{\vec p}= -\partial \mathcal H/\partial \vec q = (e/m)(\vec p - e\vec A)\times\vec B/2 + \cdots$. Eliminating $\vec p$ gives $m\ddot{\vec q} = e\, \dot{\vec q}\times \vec B$, i.e. cyclotron motion in planes perpendicular to $\vec B$ with $\vec\omega_c = (e/m)\vec B$.

(b) The Boltzmann equations with elastic spin-conserving collisions are
$\partial_t f_\uparrow - \{\mathcal H_\uparrow, f_\uparrow\} = \int d^2p_2\, d\Omega\, |v_1-v_2|\big[ (d\sigma/d\Omega)(f_\uparrow'f_\uparrow^{\prime 2} - f_\uparrow f_\uparrow^2) + (d\sigma_\times/d\Omega)(f_\uparrow'f_\downarrow' - f_\uparrow f_\downarrow)\big]$, and similarly for $\downarrow$ with $\uparrow\leftrightarrow\downarrow$.

(c) Define $H=\int d^2q\,d^2p\, [f_\uparrow\ln f_\uparrow + f_\downarrow\ln f_\downarrow]$. Using the standard symmetrization of the collision integrals, one finds each contribution is non-positive, hence $dH/dt\le 0$.

(d) If at each $\vec q$, $\ln f_\alpha(\vec p)$ is a linear combination of collision invariants, then for every binary collision the sums of $\ln f$ before and after are equal, making each collision contribution vanish; thus $dH/dt=0$.

(e) If $f_\alpha=f_\alpha(I_1,I_2,\dots)$ depends only on invariants $I_j$ with $\{I_j,\mathcal H_\alpha\}=0$, then the streaming term vanishes: $\{f_\alpha,\mathcal H_\alpha\}=\sum_j (\partial f_\alpha/\partial I_j)\{I_j,\mathcal H_\alpha\}=0$.

(f) During collisions at fixed $\vec q$, momentum conservation implies $\vec q\times(\vec p_1+\vec p_2)=\vec q\times(\vec p'_1+\vec p'_2)$ so $\vec L$ is conserved. Away from collisions, for the given $\mathcal H$, $\{\mathcal H,\vec q\times\vec p\}=0$; in 2D the conserved component is $L_z$.

(g) The most general equilibrium form compatible with (e) and collisions is $f_\alpha = A_\alpha \exp[-\beta \mathcal H_\alpha - \gamma L_z]$ (possible rigid rotation via $\gamma$).

(h) Impurities break momentum (and $L_z$) conservation, removing the $\gamma$ term. Magnetic impurities mix spins implying $A_\uparrow=A_\downarrow$; non-magnetic do not.

(i) Angular momentum conservation does not yield an independent hydrodynamic equation beyond momentum; spin conservation yields a new equation for magnetization (spin density difference).
final_answers: ["$m\\ddot{\\vec q}= e\\, \\dot{\\vec q}\\times\\vec B$ (cyclotron motion)", "Boltzmann equations with collision integrals for $\\sigma$ and $\\sigma_\\times$ as written", "$dH/dt\\le 0$", "$dH/dt=0$ when $\\ln f$ is a linear combination of collision invariants", "Streaming term $\\{f_\\alpha,\\mathcal H_\\alpha\\}=0$ for $f_\\alpha=f_\\alpha(I_j)$ with $\\{I_j,\\mathcal H_\\alpha\\}=0", "Angular momentum (in 2D, $L_z$) is conserved in and between collisions", "$f_\\alpha = A_\\alpha e^{-\\beta\\mathcal H_\\alpha - \\gamma L_z}$", "Remove $\\gamma$; magnetic impurities enforce $A_\\uparrow=A_\\downarrow$", "Spin adds a new hydrodynamic equation; angular momentum does not"]

question:
2. Surfactants: Surfactant molecules such as those in soap or shampoo prefer to spread on the air-water surface rather than dissolve in water. To see this, float a hair on the surface of water and gently touch the water in its vicinity with a piece of soap. (This is also why a piece of soap can power a toy paper boat.)

(a) The air-water surface tension $\mathcal{S}_0$ (assumed to be temperature independent) is reduced roughly by $\frac{N k_B T}{A}$, where $N$ is the number of surfactant particles, and $A$ is the area. Explain this result qualitatively.

(b) Place a drop of water on a clean surface. Observe what happens to the air-water-surface contact angle as you gently touch the droplet surface with a small piece of soap, and explain the observation.

(c) More careful observations show that at higher surfactant densities
$$
\left. \frac{\partial \mathcal{S}}{\partial A} \right|_{T} = \frac{N k_B T}{(A - N b)^2} - \frac{2a}{A} \left( \frac{N}{A} \right)^2 ,\qquad \left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial \mathcal{S}} \right|_{A} = -\frac{A - N b}{N k_B};
$$
where $a$ and $b$ are constants. Obtain the expression for $\mathcal{S}(A, T)$ and explain qualitatively the origin of the corrections described by $a$ and $b$.

(d) Find an expression for $C_{\mathcal{S}} - C_A$ in terms of $\left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial A}\right|_{T}$, $\mathcal{S}$, $\left.\frac{\partial \mathcal{S}}{\partial A}\right|_{T}$, and $\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial \mathcal{S}}\right|_{A}$, for $\left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right|_A = \left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right|_{\mathcal{S}}$.
solution:
(a) Typical surfactants have hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails and accumulate at the interface, behaving like a 2D gas. Their interfacial osmotic pressure lowers the free energy upon increasing area, giving
$$
\Delta F_{\text{surf}} = \frac{N}{A} k_B T\,\Delta A = (\mathcal{S} - \mathcal{S}_0)\,\Delta A \implies \mathcal{S} = \mathcal{S}_0 - \frac{N}{A} k_B T.
$$

(b) Young's equation for the contact angle is
$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\mathcal{S}_{as} - \mathcal{S}_{ws}}{\mathcal{S}_{aw}}.
$$
Touching the interface with soap lowers $\mathcal{S}_{aw}$, hence $\cos\theta$ increases and $\theta$ decreases; the drop spreads more.

(c) With interactions at high surface density one finds
$$
\left. \frac{\partial \mathcal{S}}{\partial A} \right|_T = \frac{N k_B T}{(A - N b)^2} - \frac{2a}{A} \left( \frac{N}{A} \right)^2,
\qquad
\left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial \mathcal{S}} \right|_A = -\frac{A - N b}{N k_B}.
$$
Integrating the first relation at fixed $T$ and comparing with the second gives
$$
\mathcal{S}(A,T) = \mathcal{S}_0 - \frac{N k_B T}{A - N b} + a\left(\frac{N}{A}\right)^2.
$$
The term $Nb$ accounts for excluded area (finite size of surfactants). The $a(N/A)^2$ term accounts for pair interactions; attractive interactions give $a>0$.

(d) Taking $A$ and $T$ as independent,
$$
\delta Q = dE - \mathcal{S} dA = \left(\left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial A}\right|_T - \mathcal{S}\right) dA + \left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right|_A dT.
$$
Hence
$$
C_A = \left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right|_A,\qquad C_{\mathcal{S}} = \left(\left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial A}\right|_T - \mathcal{S}\right)\left.\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right|_{\mathcal{S}} + \left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right|_{\mathcal{S}}.
$$
Using the chain rule $\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial \mathcal{S}}\right|_A \left.\frac{\partial \mathcal{S}}{\partial A}\right|_T \left.\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right|_{\mathcal{S}} = -1$, and the given $\left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right|_A = \left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right|_{\mathcal{S}}$, we obtain
$$
C_{\mathcal{S}} - C_A = \left(\left.\frac{\partial E}{\partial A}\right|_T - \mathcal{S}\right)\left[\,-\frac{1}{\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial \mathcal{S}}\right|_A\,\left.\frac{\partial \mathcal{S}}{\partial A}\right|_T}\,\right].
$$
final_answers: [null, null, "\\mathcal{S}(A,T) = \\mathcal{S}_0 - \\dfrac{N k_B T}{A - N b} + a\\left(\\dfrac{N}{A}\\right)^2", "\\left(C_{\\mathcal{S}} - C_A\\right) = \\left(\\left.\\dfrac{\\partial E}{\\partial A}\\right|_T - \\mathcal{S}\\right)\\Big/\\left[-\\left.\\dfrac{\\partial T}{\\partial \\mathcal{S}}\\right|_A\\,\\left.\\dfrac{\\partial \\mathcal{S}}{\\partial A}\\right|_T\\right]"]

question:
Josephson junction.

Consider two superconducting quantum dots separated by a tunneling barrier. Each dot is described by
\[
S^{i}[\psi^{i}_{\alpha}, \phi_{i}] = \int_{0}^{\beta} d\tau \sum_{\alpha} \bar{\psi}^{i}_{\alpha} (\partial_{\tau} + \xi_{i\alpha} \sigma_{3} + e^{i \phi_{i}(\tau) \sigma_{3}} \Delta \sigma_{1}) \psi^{i}_{\alpha}, \quad i=1,2,
\]
with Nambu spinors and Pauli matrices in particle-hole space. The dots form a capacitor
\[
S_{\rm int} = \frac{E_C}{4} \int d\tau \, ( \hat N_1 - \hat N_2 )^2, \qquad \hat N_i = \sum_{\alpha} \bar{\psi}^i_{\alpha} \sigma_3 \psi^i_{\alpha},
\]
and are coupled by
\[
S_{\rm T} = \sum_{\alpha\beta} \int d\tau \, \bar{\psi}^1_{\alpha} (T_{\alpha\beta} \sigma_3) \psi^2_{\beta} + \text{h.c.}
\]
(a) Show that the HS action is
\[\small
S_{\mathrm{eff}}[V,\phi] = S_{\mathrm c}[V] - \operatorname{tr} \ln\big( \partial_{\tau} + (\hat{\xi}_1 + \tfrac{i}{2}(\dot{\phi}_1 + V))\sigma_3 + \Delta \sigma_1 \; e^{-\frac{i}{2}(\phi_1 - \phi_2)\sigma_3} T - T^{\dagger} e^{\frac{i}{2}(\phi_1 - \phi_2)\sigma_3} (\partial_{\tau} + (\hat{\xi}_2 + \tfrac{i}{2}(\dot{\phi}_2 - V)) \sigma_3 + \Delta \sigma_1) \big),
\]
with \(S_c[V] = \frac{1}{4E_C} \int d\tau \, V^2\). Assume the Josephson conditions \(\dot\phi_1 = -\dot\phi_2 \equiv -\dot\phi\), \(V=\dot\phi\).

(b) Expanding to second order in \(T\) and using the Josephson conditions, show that the diagonal Gorkov terms do not contribute for \(|\omega_m| \ll \Delta\) (up to small corrections of order \(\omega_m/\Delta\)). Interpret this result.

(c) Show that the off-diagonal sector yields the Josephson term
\[
S_{\text{tun}}[\Delta, \phi] = \gamma \int_0^{\beta} d\tau \, \cos\big(2\phi(\tau)\big), \qquad \gamma = |T|^2 (\pi \rho)^2 \Delta.
\]

(d) Perform a HS transformation of the charging term and derive the equations of motion. Show that the Josephson current is
\[ I = -2\gamma \sin(2\phi). \]
solution:
(a) Decoupling the capacitive interaction by a field \(V\) and integrating out fermions gives
\[
S[V, \phi] = S_c[V] - \mathrm{tr} \ln \begin{pmatrix} G_1^{-1}[V,\phi_1] & -T\sigma_3 \\ -T^{\dagger}\sigma_3 & G_2^{-1}[-V,\phi_2] \end{pmatrix},
\]
with Gorkov Green functions. Using \(e^{i\phi \sigma_3} \sigma_1 = D(\phi) \sigma_1 D^{-1}(\phi)\), where \(D(\phi) = e^{\frac{i}{2}\phi \sigma_3}\), one can factor out the phases and arrive at the stated form. Assuming the Josephson conditions \(\dot\phi_1 = -\dot\phi_2 \equiv -\dot\phi\) and \(V=\dot\phi\) simplifies the dependence to the relative phase.

(b) Expanding to second order in \(T\) gives
\[
S_{\text{tun}}[\phi] = |T|^2 \sum_{\alpha\alpha'\omega_m\omega_n} \mathrm{tr}\Big( G_{1,\alpha\omega_n} (e^{i\sigma_3\phi})_m G_{2,\alpha'\omega_{n+m}} (e^{-i\sigma_3\phi})_{-m} - (\phi\leftrightarrow 0) \Big).
\]
Writing \(G_i = G_{i,d} + G_{i,o}\), one finds that the contribution from \(G_{i,d}\) vanishes for \(|\omega_m| \ll \Delta\), up to corrections of order \(\omega_m/\Delta\). Physically, low-frequency phase fluctuations cannot create quasiparticles across the gap, so there is no dissipation from the diagonal sector at low frequency.

(c) Using \(G_{o,n} = -\Delta \sigma_1/(\omega_n^2 + \xi^2 + \Delta^2)\), neglecting \(\mathcal O(|\omega_m|/\Delta)\) terms, and integrating over energies gives
\[
S_{\text{tun}}[\phi] = |T|^2 (\pi \rho)^2 \Delta \int d\tau \, \cos(2\phi(\tau)) \equiv \gamma \int d\tau \, \cos(2\phi(\tau)).
\]

(d) HS decoupling of \(\dot\phi^2/(4E_C)\) introduces the conjugate \(N\) and yields the Hamiltonian form
\[
S[\phi,N] = E_C \int d\tau \, N^2 + \gamma \int d\tau \, \cos(2\phi) + i \int d\tau \, N \dot\phi.
\]
The equations of motion are
\[ \dot\phi = i 2 E_C N, \qquad \dot N = 2 i \gamma \sin(2\phi). \]
The current \(I = i\partial_{\tau} N\) is thus
\[ I = -2\gamma \sin(2\phi). \]
final_answers: ["S_{\\mathrm{eff}}[V,\\phi]=S_{\\mathrm c}[V]-\\operatorname{tr}\\ln\\big(\\partial_{\\tau}+(\\hat\\xi_1+\\tfrac{i}{2}(\\dot\\phi_1+V))\\sigma_3+\\Delta\\sigma_1\\; e^{-\\frac{i}{2}(\\phi_1-\\phi_2)\\sigma_3}T - T^{\\dagger}e^{\\frac{i}{2}(\\phi_1-\\phi_2)\\sigma_3}(\\partial_{\\tau}+(\\hat\\xi_2+\\tfrac{i}{2}(\\dot\\phi_2-V))\\sigma_3+\\Delta\\sigma_1)\\big)", null, "S_{\\text{tun}}[\\Delta,\\phi] = \\gamma \\int_0^{\\beta} d\\tau \\, \\cos(2\\phi(\\tau)),\\; \\gamma = |T|^2 (\\pi \\rho)^2 \\Delta", "I = -2\\gamma \\sin(2\\phi)"]

question:
Gauge fluctuations in superconductors: The Landau-Ginzburg model of superconductivity describes a complex superconducting order parameter \(\Psi(\mathbf{x}) = \Psi_1(\mathbf{x}) + i\Psi_2(\mathbf{x})\), and the electromagnetic vector potential \(\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x})\), which are subject to a Hamiltonian
\[\beta H = \int d^3\mathbf{x} \left[ \frac{t}{2}|\Psi|^2 + u|\Psi|^4 + \frac{K}{2} D_\mu\Psi\, D_\mu^*\Psi^* + \frac{L}{2} (\nabla \times \mathbf{A})^2 \right].\]
The gauge-invariant derivative \(D_\mu = \partial_\mu - ieA_\mu(\mathbf{x})\), introduces the coupling between the two fields. (In terms of Cooper pair parameters, \(e=e^*c/\hbar\), \(K=\hbar^2/2m^*\).)

(a) Show that the above Hamiltonian is invariant under the local gauge symmetry:
\[\Psi(\mathbf{x}) \to \Psi(\mathbf{x}) e^{i\theta(\mathbf{x})}, \quad A_\mu(\mathbf{x}) \to A_\mu(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{1}{e}\partial_\mu\theta.\]

(b) Show that there is a saddle point solution of the form \(\Psi(\mathbf{x}) = \bar{\Psi}\), and \(\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x}) = 0\), and find \(\bar{\Psi}\) for \(t>0\) and \(t<0\).

(c) For \(t<0\), calculate the cost of fluctuations by setting
\[\begin{cases} \Psi(\mathbf{x}) = (\bar{\Psi} + \phi(\mathbf{x})) \, e^{i\theta(\mathbf{x})}, \\ A_\mu(\mathbf{x}) = a_\mu(\mathbf{x}), \quad (\partial_\mu a_\mu = 0 \text{ Coulomb gauge}) \end{cases}\]
and expanding \(\beta H\) to quadratic order in \(\phi, \theta\), and \(\mathbf{a}\).

(d) Perform a Fourier transformation, and calculate the expectation values of \(\langle |\phi(\mathbf{q})|^2 \rangle\), \(\langle |\theta(\mathbf{q})|^2 \rangle\), and \(\langle |\mathbf{a}(\mathbf{q})|^2 \rangle\).
solution:
(a) Under the local gauge transformation,
\[\Psi\to \Psi e^{i\theta},\quad A_\mu \to A_\mu + \frac{1}{e}\partial_\mu\theta,\]
we have
\[(\partial_\mu - ieA_\mu - i\partial_\mu\theta)(\Psi e^{i\theta}) = e^{i\theta}(\partial_\mu - ieA_\mu)\Psi = e^{i\theta} D_\mu\Psi,\]
and \(\nabla\times\frac{1}{e}\nabla\theta=0\). Thus \(D_\mu\Psi D_\mu^*\Psi^*\) and \((\nabla\times\mathbf{A})^2\) are invariant; the potential terms depend only on \(|\Psi|\). Hence \(\beta H\) is gauge invariant.

(b) The saddle-point equations are
\[\frac{\delta H}{\delta \Psi^*}=0: \quad \frac{t}{2}\Psi + 2u\Psi|\Psi|^2 - \frac{K}{2} D_\mu D_\mu \Psi = 0,\]
\[\frac{\delta H}{\delta A_\mu}=0: \quad \frac{K}{2}(-ie\Psi D_\mu^*\Psi^* + ie\Psi^* D_\mu\Psi) - L\,\epsilon_{\alpha\beta\mu}\epsilon_{\alpha\gamma\delta}\partial_\beta\partial_\gamma A_\delta = 0.
\]
The uniform ansatz \(\Psi=\bar\Psi\) (real), \(\mathbf{A}=0\) solves these. The first reduces to
\[\frac{t}{2}\bar\Psi + 4u\bar\Psi|\bar\Psi|^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \bar\Psi=0 \; (t>0),\quad |\bar\Psi|^2 = -\frac{t}{4u} \; (t<0).
\]

(c) Write \(\Psi=(\bar\Psi+\phi)e^{i\theta}\), \(\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{a}\), Coulomb gauge \(\partial_\mu a_\mu=0\). Expanding to quadratic order,
\[D_\mu\Psi D_\mu^*\Psi^* \Rightarrow \bar\Psi^2(\nabla\theta)^2 + (\nabla\phi)^2 - 2e\bar\Psi^2 a_\mu\partial_\mu\theta + e^2 \bar\Psi^2 |\mathbf{a}|^2.
\]
The mixed term integrates to zero by parts and \(\partial_\mu a_\mu=0\). Thus
\[\beta H^{(2)} = \int d^3x\,\Big\{ \Big(\tfrac{t}{2}+6u\bar\Psi^2\Big)\phi^2 + \tfrac{K}{2}(\nabla\phi)^2 + \tfrac{K}{2}\bar\Psi^2(\nabla\theta)^2 + \tfrac{K}{2}e^2\bar\Psi^2 |\mathbf{a}|^2 + \tfrac{L}{2}(\nabla\times\mathbf{a})^2 \Big\}.
\]

(d) Fourier transforming and using \(\mathbf{q}\perp\mathbf{a}(\mathbf{q})\) so that \(|\mathbf{q}\times\mathbf{a}|^2=q^2|\mathbf{a}|^2\),
\[\beta H^{(2)} = \sum_{\mathbf{q}} \Big\{ \Big( \tfrac{t}{2}+6u\bar\Psi^2 + \tfrac{K}{2}q^2 \Big) |\phi(\mathbf{q})|^2 + \tfrac{K}{2}\bar\Psi^2 q^2 |\theta(\mathbf{q})|^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\big( Ke^2\bar\Psi^2 + L q^2 \big) |\mathbf{a}(\mathbf{q})|^2 \Big\}.
\]
For \(t<0\), with \(|\bar\Psi|^2=-t/(4u)\), the Gaussian averages are
\[\langle|\phi(\mathbf{q})|^2\rangle = \frac{1}{K q^2 - 2t},\quad \langle|\theta(\mathbf{q})|^2\rangle = \frac{4u}{K t\, q^2},\quad \langle|\mathbf{a}(\mathbf{q})|^2\rangle = \frac{2}{L q^2 - K e^2 t/(4u)}\; (\text{2 transverse components}).\]
The gauge field acquires a mass via the Higgs mechanism.
final_answers: [null, "\\bar\\Psi=0\\; (t>0),\\; |\\bar\\Psi|^2=-t/(4u)\\; (t<0);\\; \\mathbf{A}=0", "\\beta H^{(2)} = \\int d^3x\\,\\{ (\\tfrac{t}{2}+6u\\bar\\Psi^2)\\phi^2 + \\tfrac{K}{2}(\\nabla\\phi)^2 + \\tfrac{K}{2}\\bar\\Psi^2(\\nabla\\theta)^2 + \\tfrac{K}{2}e^2\\bar\\Psi^2|\\mathbf{a}|^2 + \\tfrac{L}{2}(\\nabla\\times\\mathbf{a})^2 \\}", "\\langle|\\phi(\\mathbf{q})|^2\\rangle = 1/(Kq^2-2t),\\; \\langle|\\theta(\\mathbf{q})|^2\\rangle = 4u/(K t q^2),\\; \\langle|\\mathbf{a}(\\mathbf{q})|^2\\rangle = 2/(Lq^2 - K e^2 t/4u)"]

question:
Migdal-Kadanoff method: Consider Potts spins s_i = (1, 2, \dots, q), on sites i of a hypercubic lattice, interacting with their nearest neighbors via a Hamiltonian
$$ -\beta\mathcal{H} = K \sum_{<ij>} \delta_{s_i,s_j}. $$
(a) In d = 1 find the exact recursion relations by a b = 2 renormalization/decimation process. Indentify all fixed points and note their stability.
(b) Write down the recursion relation K'(K) in d-dimensions for b = 2, using the Migdal-Kadanoff bond moving scheme.
(c) By considering the stability of the fixed points at zero and infinite coupling, prove the existence of a non-trivial fixed point at finite K* for d > 1.
(d) For d = 2, obtain K* and y_t, for q = 3, 1, and 0.
solution:
(a) Decimating every other spin in d=1 gives
$$ \sum_{s_1=1}^q e^{K(\delta_{\sigma_1 s_1} + \delta_{s_1 \sigma_2})} = \begin{cases} q-1+e^{2K} & (\sigma_1 = \sigma_2) \\ q-2+2e^K & (\sigma_1 \neq \sigma_2) \end{cases} = e^{g'+K'\delta_{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}}, $$
leading to
$$ e^{K'} = \frac{q-1+e^{2K}}{q-2+2e^K}, \quad e^{g'} = q-2+2e^K. $$
Fixed points: setting K'=K gives x=e^{K^*} solving x = (q-1+x^2)/(q-2+2x), i.e.
$$ x^2 + (q-2)x - (q-1)=0 \;\Rightarrow\; x=1 \; (K^*=0). $$
For K\ll 1, K'\approx K^2/q \ll K so K^*=0 is stable. K^*\to\infty is also a fixed point; for K\gg 1,
$$ e^{K'} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} e^K \Rightarrow K'=K-\ln 2 < K, $$
so K^*\to\infty is unstable in 1D.

(b) In the Migdal-Kadanoff approximation, bond moving multiplies couplings by 2^{d-1}, then decimation yields
$$ e^{K'} = \frac{q-1+e^{2\times 2^{d-1}K}}{q-2+2e^{2^{d-1}K}}. $$

(c) Near K=0, K' \approx 2^{2d-2}K^2/q \ll K, so K^*=0 is stable. For K\to\infty,
$$ e^{K'} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\exp[(2^d-2^{d-1})K] \Rightarrow K' = 2^{d-1}K - \ln 2, $$
which implies K'\gg K for d>1; thus K^*\to\infty becomes stable for d>1. Therefore, by continuity there exists a finite non-trivial fixed point K^* separating flows to the two stable fixed points.

(d) In d=2:
- q=3: with x=e^{K},
$$ x = \frac{2+x^4}{1+2x^2} \;\Rightarrow\; x^4-2x^3-x+2=(x-2)(x^3-1)=0, $$
non-trivial solution x=2 gives K^*=\ln 2 \approx 0.69. Thermal exponent from
$$ 2^{y_t} = \left.\frac{\partial K'}{\partial K}\right|_{K^*} = 4\left[ \frac{e^{4K^*}}{e^{4K^*}+2} - \frac{e^{2K^*}}{1+2e^{2K^*}} \right] = \frac{16}{9}, $$
so y_t \approx 0.83.
- q\to 1: analytic continuation gives
$$ e^{K'} = \frac{e^{4K}}{-1+2e^{2K}}. $$
Fixed point solves x = x^4/(-1+2x^2) \Rightarrow (x-1)(x^2-x-1)=0, giving x=\tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\approx 1.62, i.e. K^*\approx 0.48. Then
$$ 2^{y_t} = 4(1-e^{-K^*}) \Rightarrow y_t \approx 0.61. $$
- q\to 0: here
$$ e^{K'} = \frac{-1+e^{4K}}{-2+2e^{2K}}, $$
so x = (-1+x^4)/(-2+2x^2) \Rightarrow (x-1)^3(x+1)=0; only finite solution x=1 (K^*=0). For K\ll 1,
$$ K' \approx K + \tfrac{1}{2}K^2, $$
indicating marginal stability (y_t=0). For K\to\infty, K' = 2K - \ln 2 \gg K, so K^*\to\infty is stable.

question:
Quantum harmonic oscillators: Consider N independent quantum oscillators subject to a Hamiltonian
\[\mathcal{H}(\{n_i\}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \hbar \omega \left( n_i + \frac{1}{2} \right),\]
where \(n_i = 0, 1, 2, \dots\), is the quantum occupation number for the i-th oscillator.
(a) Calculate the entropy \(S\), as a function of the total energy \(E\). (Hint: \(\Omega(E)\) can be regarded as the number of ways of rearranging \(M = \sum_i n_i\) balls, and \(N-1\) partitions along a line.)

(b) Calculate the energy \(E\), and heat capacity \(C\), as functions of temperature \(T\), and \(N\).

(c) Find the probability \(p(n)\) that a particular oscillator is in its n-th quantum level.

(d) Comment on the difference between heat capacities for classical and quantum oscillators.
solution:
(a) With \(M \equiv \sum_i n_i = E/(\hbar\omega) - N/2\), the number of integer partitions is
\[\Omega = \frac{(M+N-1)!}{M!\,(N-1)!}.\]
Using Stirling for large arguments,
\[S = k_B \ln \Omega \approx k_B\Big[(M+N-1)\ln\tfrac{M+N-1}{e} - M\ln\tfrac{M}{e} - (N-1)\ln\tfrac{N-1}{e}\Big].\]

(b) The temperature follows from
\[\frac{1}{T} = \left.\frac{\partial S}{\partial E}\right|_{N} = \frac{k_B}{\hbar\omega} \ln\left( \frac{M+N-1}{M} \right) \approx \frac{k_B}{\hbar\omega} \ln\left( \frac{E + \tfrac{N}{2}\hbar\omega}{E - \tfrac{N}{2}\hbar\omega} \right).
\]
Inverting gives
\[E = N\hbar\omega\left[\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{e^{\hbar\omega/(k_B T)}-1}\right],\]
so that
\[C = \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_{N} = N k_B \left(\frac{\hbar\omega}{k_B T}\right)^2 \frac{e^{\hbar\omega/(k_B T)}}{\big(e^{\hbar\omega/(k_B T)}-1\big)^2}.
\]

(c) The probability for a given oscillator to occupy level \(n\) is
\[
p(n) = \exp\Big(-n\tfrac{\hbar\omega}{k_B T}\Big)\Big[1 - e^{-\hbar\omega/(k_B T)}\Big].
\]

(d) At high temperature \(\hbar\omega\ll k_B T\), \(C \to N k_B\), matching the classical value. At low temperature \(k_B T \ll \hbar\omega\), the heat capacity is exponentially small due to level spacing, with \(C \sim N k_B (\hbar\omega/(k_B T))^2 e^{-\hbar\omega/(k_B T)}\).
final_answers: ["S(E) \\approx k_B\\Big[(M+N-1)\\ln\\tfrac{M+N-1}{e} - M\\ln\\tfrac{M}{e} - (N-1)\\ln\\tfrac{N-1}{e}\\Big],\\ M=\\tfrac{E}{\\hbar\\omega}-\\tfrac{N}{2}", "E = N\\hbar\\omega\\Big[\\tfrac{1}{2} + (e^{\\hbar\\omega/(k_B T)}-1)^{-1}\\Big],\\ C = N k_B (\\hbar\\omega/(k_B T))^2 \\dfrac{e^{\\hbar\\omega/(k_B T)}}{(e^{\\hbar\\omega/(k_B T)}-1)^2}", "p(n) = e^{-n\\hbar\\omega/(k_B T)}\\big[1 - e^{-\\hbar\\omega/(k_B T)}\\big]", null]

question:
11. Clock model duality: Consider spins \(s_i=(1,2,\dots,q)\) on the sites of a square lattice, interacting via the clock model
$$
\beta H_C = - \sum_{\langle i,j \rangle} J\big(|(s_i - s_j)_{\text{mod }q}|\big).
$$

(a) Change from N site variables to 2N bond variables \(b_{ij} = (s_i - s_j)_{\text{mod }q}\). Show that the difference in the number of variables can be accounted for by the constraint that around each plaquette the sum of the four bond variables must be zero modulo q.

(b) Implement the constraints via
$$
\delta\big[S_p\big|_{\text{mod }q}\big] = \frac{1}{q} \sum_{n_p=1}^q \exp\left[ \frac{2\pi i n_p S_p}{q} \right]
$$
for each plaquette. Show that after summing over bond variables, the partition function can be written in terms of dual variables as
$$
Z = q^{-N} \sum_{\{n_p\}} \prod_{\langle p,p' \rangle} \lambda(n_p - n_{p'}) = \sum_{\{n_p\}} \exp\left[ \sum_{\langle p,p' \rangle} \tilde{J}(n_p - n_{p'}) \right],
$$
where \(\lambda(k)\) is the discrete Fourier transform of \(\mathrm{e}^{J(n)}\).

(c) Calculate the dual interaction parameter of a Potts model, and hence locate the critical point \(J_c(q)\).

(d) Construct the dual of the anisotropic Potts model with
$$
-\beta H = \sum_{x,y} \big( J_x \delta_{s_{x,y}, s_{x+1,y}} + J_y \delta_{s_{x,y}, s_{x,y+1}} \big),
$$
and find the line of self-dual interactions in the \((J_x,J_y)\) plane.
solution:
(a) For each plaquette p, the oriented sum of bond differences around it vanishes modulo q:
$$
S_p = \sum_{\text{around }p} b'_{ij} = \sum_{\text{around }p} (s_i - s_j) \equiv 0 \; (\mathrm{mod}\; q),
$$
so the 2N bond variables are constrained by N such conditions.

(b) The partition function in bond variables with constraints is
$$
Z = \sum_{\{b_{ij}\}} \prod_p \delta\big[S_p|_{\text{mod }q}\big] \prod_{\langle ij \rangle} \mathrm{e}^{J(b_{ij})} = \sum_{\{b_{ij}\}} \prod_p \frac{1}{q} \sum_{n_p} \exp\left[ \frac{2\pi i n_p}{q} \sum b'_{ij} \right] \prod_{\langle ij \rangle} \mathrm{e}^{J(b_{ij})}.
$$
Each bond \(b_{ij}\) appears in the sums for its two adjacent plaquettes p and p', producing
$$
\sum_{b_{ij}=0}^{q-1} \exp\left[ \frac{2\pi i b_{ij}}{q} (n_p - n_{p'}) + J(b_{ij}) \right] = \lambda(n_p - n_{p'}) \equiv \mathrm{e}^{\tilde{J}(n_p - n_{p'})}.
$$
Thus
$$
Z = q^{-N} \sum_{\{n_p\}} \prod_{\langle p,p' \rangle} \mathrm{e}^{\tilde{J}(n_p - n_{p'})} = \sum_{\{n_p\}} \exp\left[ \sum_{\langle p,p' \rangle} \tilde{J}(n_p - n_{p'}) \right].
$$

(c) For the Potts model, the transform has two values:
$$
\lambda(n_p = n_{p'}) = \mathrm{e}^J + q - 1,\qquad \lambda(n_p \ne n_{p'}) = \mathrm{e}^J - 1.
$$
Hence the dual coupling satisfies
$$
\mathrm{e}^{\tilde{J}} = \frac{\mathrm{e}^J + q - 1}{\mathrm{e}^J - 1}.
$$
A single critical point occurs at self-duality, yielding
$$
J_c(q) = \ln\big(1 + \sqrt{q-1}\big).
$$

(d) The dual of the anisotropic square lattice maps \(J_x\leftrightarrow \tilde{J}_y\) and \(J_y\leftrightarrow \tilde{J}_x\) with
$$
\tilde{J}_x = \ln \frac{\mathrm{e}^{J_y} + q - 1}{\mathrm{e}^{J_y} - 1},\qquad \tilde{J}_y = \ln \frac{\mathrm{e}^{J_x} + q - 1}{\mathrm{e}^{J_x} - 1}.
$$
The self-dual line is \(\tilde{J}_y = J_x\) (equivalently \(\tilde{J}_x = J_y\)).
final_answers: ["Constraint: \\(\\sum_{\\text{around }p} b'_{ij} \\equiv 0\\; (\\mathrm{mod}\\;q)\\)", "Z = q^{-N} \\sum_{\\{n_p\\}} \\prod_{\\langle p,p' \\rangle} \\lambda(n_p - n_{p'}) = \\sum_{\\{n_p\\}} \\exp\\big[ \\sum_{\\langle p,p' \\rangle} \\tilde{J}(n_p - n_{p'}) \\big]", "\\mathrm{e}^{\\tilde{J}} = (\\mathrm{e}^J + q - 1)/(\\mathrm{e}^J - 1);\\; J_c(q) = \\ln(1+\\sqrt{q-1})", "\\tilde{J}_x = \\ln \\frac{\\mathrm{e}^{J_y}+q-1}{\\mathrm{e}^{J_y}-1},\\; \\tilde{J}_y = \\ln \\frac{\\mathrm{e}^{J_x}+q-1}{\\mathrm{e}^{J_x}-1};\\; \\text{self-dual line: } \\tilde{J}_y=J_x"]

question:
Two identical spin ½ fermions move in one dimension under the influence of the infinite-wall potential $V = ∞$ for $x < 0, x > L$, and $V = 0$ for $0 ≤ x ≤ L$.
(a) Write the ground-state wave function and the ground-state energy when the two particles are constrained to a triplet spin state (ortho state).
(b) Repeat (a) when they are in a singlet spin state (para state).
(c) Let us now suppose that the two particles interact mutually via a very short-range attractive potential that can be approximated by
$$
V = −λδ(x_1 - x_2) \quad (λ > 0).
$$
Assuming that perturbation theory is valid even with such a singular potential, discuss semiquantitatively what happens to the energy levels obtained in (a) and (b).
solution:
The single particle wave function is $\psi_n(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}} \sin(n\pi x/L)$, and the single particle energy is $E_n = n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2 / 2mL^2$. The triplet spin state is symmetric, so in this case, the spatial state must be antisymmetric. Therefore (with $H = p_1^2/2m + p_2^2/2m$),
$$
\psi_{\rm gs}(x_1, x_2)|_{\rm triplet} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} [\psi_1(x_1)\psi_2(x_2) - \psi_1(x_2)\psi_2(x_1)]
$$
$$
H \psi_{\rm gs}(x_1, x_2)|_{\rm triplet} = \left[\frac{\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2mL^2} + \frac{4\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2mL^2}\right] \psi_{\rm gs}(x_1, x_2)|_{\rm triplet} = \frac{5\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2mL^2} \psi_{\rm gs}(x_1, x_2)|_{\rm triplet}
$$
For the singlet spin state, the spatial state is symmetric, so
$$
\psi_{\rm gs}(x_1, x_2)|_{\rm singlet} = \psi_1(x_1)\psi_1(x_2)
$$
$$
H \psi_{\rm gs}(x_1, x_2)|_{\rm singlet} = \left[\frac{\pi^2\hbar^2}{2mL^2} + \frac{\pi^2\hbar^2}{2mL^2}\right] \psi_{\rm gs}(x_1, x_2)|_{\rm singlet} = \frac{\pi^2\hbar^2}{mL^2} \psi_{\rm gs}(x_1, x_2)|_{\rm singlet}
$$
In first order perturbation theory, the energy shift is
$$
\Delta E = \langle \text{gs}|V|\text{gs}\rangle = \int dx_1 \int dx_2 \ \psi_{\text{gs}}(x_1, x_2) V(x_1, x_2) \psi_{\text{gs}}(x_1, x_2) = -\lambda \int dx \ \psi_{\text{gs}}^2(x, x)
$$
In the triplet case, obviously $\Delta E = 0$. The antisymmetric spatial wave function never allows the two particles to be at the same place, so they never feel the $\delta$-function potential. On the other hand, in the singlet case,
$$
\Delta E_{\text{singlet}} = -\lambda \left(\frac{2}{L}\right)^2 \int_0^L \sin^4\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right) dx = -\lambda \left(\frac{2}{L}\right)^2 \frac{3L}{8} = -\frac{3\lambda}{2L}
$$
final_answers: ["\\frac{5\\pi^2 \\hbar^2}{2mL^2}", "\\frac{\\pi^2 \\hbar^2}{mL^2}", null]

question:
5. Crumpled surfaces: The configurations of a crumpled sheet of paper can be described by a vector field $\vec r(x)$, denoting the position in three dimensional space, $\vec r=(r_1,r_2,r_3)$, of the point at location $x=(x_1,x_2)$ on the flat sheet. The energy of each configuration is assumed to be invariant under translations and rotations of the sheet of paper.
(a) Show that the two lowest order (in derivatives) terms in the quadratic part of a Landau-Ginzburg Hamiltonian for this system are:
$$ \beta H_0[\vec r] = \int d^2 x \sum_{\alpha=1,2} \left[ \frac{t}{2}\,\partial_\alpha\vec r\cdot\partial_\alpha\vec r + \frac{K}{2}\, \partial_\alpha^2\vec r\cdot\partial_\alpha^2\vec r \right]. $$
(b) Write down the lowest order terms (there are two) that appear at the quartic level.
(c) Discuss what happens when $t$ changes sign, assuming that quartic terms provide the required stability (and $K>0$).
solution:
(a) Translation invariance forbids explicit dependence on $\vec r$; the Hamiltonian can depend only on its derivatives. Rotational invariance in embedding space requires contractions over Cartesian components, and isotropy on the sheet requires contracted spatial derivatives. The lowest-order quadratic invariants are therefore
$$ \beta H_0[\vec r] = \int d^2 x \left[ \frac{t}{2}\,\partial_\alpha r_i\,\partial_\alpha r_i + \frac{K}{2}\,\partial_\alpha\partial_\beta r_i\,\partial_\alpha\partial_\beta r_i \right], $$
which is equivalent to the stated form.

(b) The two independent quartic invariants constructed from gradients are
$$ \beta H'[\vec r] = \int d^2 x \left[ u\, (\partial_\alpha r_i\,\partial_\alpha r_i)(\partial_\beta r_j\,\partial_\beta r_j) + v\,(\partial_\alpha r_i\,\partial_\beta r_i)(\partial_\alpha r_j\,\partial_\beta r_j) \right]. $$

(c) The structure parallels a Landau-Ginzburg theory with order parameter $\partial_\alpha r_i$. For $t>0$ the minimum is at $\partial_\alpha r_i=0$ (crumpled state). When $t<0$, a nonzero gradient minimizes the energy (stretched/opened sheet), with the quartic terms stabilizing the magnitude; rotational symmetry leaves the direction spontaneously chosen.
final_answers: ["\\beta H_0[\\vec r] = \\int d^2 x\\,\\sum_{\\alpha=1,2}\\big[\\tfrac{t}{2}\\,\\partial_\\alpha\\vec r\\cdot\\partial_\\alpha\\vec r + \\tfrac{K}{2}\\,\\partial_\\alpha^2\\vec r\\cdot\\partial_\\alpha^2\\vec r\\big]", "u\\,(\\partial_\\alpha r_i\\,\\partial_\\alpha r_i)(\\partial_\\beta r_j\\,\\partial_\\beta r_j)\\; \\text{and}\\; v\\,(\\partial_\\alpha r_i\\,\\partial_\\beta r_i)(\\partial_\\alpha r_j\\,\\partial_\\beta r_j)", null]

question:
Dirac fermions are non-interacting particles of spin $1/2$. The one-particle states come in pairs of positive and negative energies, $\mathcal{E}_{\pm}(\vec{k}) = \pm \sqrt{m^{2}c^{4} + \hbar^{2}k^{2}c^{2}}$, independent of spin.

(a) For any fermionic system of chemical potential $\mu$, show that the probability of finding an occupied state of energy $\mu + \delta$ is the same as that of finding an unoccupied state of energy $\mu - \delta$ ($\delta$ is any constant energy).

(b) At zero temperature all negative energy Dirac states are occupied and all positive energy ones are empty, i.e. $\mu(T=0)=0$. Using the result in (a) find the chemical potential at finite temperatures $T$.

(c) Show that the mean excitation energy of this system at finite temperature satisfies
$\displaystyle E(T) - E(0) = 4V \int \frac{d^{3} \vec{k}}{(2\pi)^{3}} \frac{\mathcal{E}_+(\vec{k})}{\exp\big(\beta \mathcal{E}_+(\vec{k})\big) + 1}$.

(d) Evaluate the integral in part (c) for massless Dirac particles ($m=0$).

(e) Calculate the heat capacity, $C_V$, of such massless Dirac particles.

(f) Describe the qualitative dependence of the heat capacity at low temperature if the particles are massive.
solution:
(a) For a level of energy $\mathcal{E}$, Fermi statistics gives $P(n=1) = e^{\beta(\mu-\mathcal{E})}/(1+e^{\beta(\mu-\mathcal{E})})$, $P(n=0)=1/(1+e^{\beta(\mu-\mathcal{E})})$. Hence for $\mu+\delta$, $P(n=1) = 1/(1+e^{-\beta\delta})$, while for $\mu-\delta$, $P(n=0)=1/(1+e^{-\beta\delta})$. Thus $P[\text{occ at }\mu+\delta]=P[\text{empty at }\mu-\delta]$.

(b) With particle-hole symmetry, for $\mu=0$ the total number (occupied negative plus empty positive) is unchanged with $T$; therefore $\mu(T)=0$ for all $T$.

(c) Labeling positive/negative branches by $\pm$ and using spin degeneracy, the excitation energy is
$E(T)-E(0) = 2\sum_{\vec{k}}\big[\langle n_+(\vec{k})\rangle \mathcal{E}_+(\vec{k}) - (1-\langle n_-(\vec{k})\rangle) \mathcal{E}_-(\vec{k})\big] = 4V \int \dfrac{d^3k}{(2\pi)^3} \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_+(\vec{k})}{e^{\beta\mathcal{E}_+(\vec{k})}+1}$.

(d) For $m=0$, $\mathcal{E}_+(k)=\hbar c k$, and
$E(T)-E(0) = 4V \int_0^{\infty} \dfrac{4\pi k^2 dk}{8\pi^3} \dfrac{\hbar c k}{e^{\beta \hbar c k}+1} = \dfrac{2V}{\pi^2} k_B T \left(\dfrac{k_B T}{\hbar c}\right)^3 \int_0^{\infty} dx \dfrac{x^3}{e^x+1} = \dfrac{7\pi^2}{60}\, V k_B T \left(\dfrac{k_B T}{\hbar c}\right)^3$.

(e) $C_V = \partial E/\partial T|_V = \dfrac{7\pi^2}{15}\, V k_B \left(\dfrac{k_B T}{\hbar c}\right)^3$.

(f) For $m\ne 0$, there is a gap, so excitations are activated. Near threshold, $\mathcal{E}_+(k)\approx mc^2 + \hbar^2 k^2/(2m)$. Then to leading order,
$E(T)-E(0) \propto V\, \lambda^{-3} \,(mc^2) e^{-\beta mc^2}$ and $C(T) \propto k_B\, V\, \lambda^{-3} (\beta mc^2)^2 e^{-\beta mc^2}$, where $\lambda = h/\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}$.
final_answers: ["$P(\\text{occ at }\\mu+\\delta)=P(\\text{empty at }\\mu-\\delta)$", "$\\mu(T)=0$", "$E(T)-E(0) = 4V \\int \\dfrac{d^3k}{(2\\pi)^3} \\dfrac{\\mathcal{E}_+(\\vec{k})}{e^{\\beta\\mathcal{E}_+(\\vec{k})}+1}$", "$E(T)-E(0) = \\dfrac{7\\pi^2}{60}\\, V k_B T \\big(\\dfrac{k_B T}{\\hbar c}\\big)^3$", "$C_V = \\dfrac{7\\pi^2}{15}\\, V k_B \\big(\\dfrac{k_B T}{\\hbar c}\\big)^3$", "Activated: $C \\propto (\\beta mc^2)^2 e^{-\\beta mc^2}\\, V/\\lambda^3$"]

question:
Hard sphere gas: Consider a gas of N hard spheres in a box. A single sphere occupies volume \(\omega\), while its center of mass can explore a volume \(V\) (if the box is otherwise empty). There are no other interactions between the spheres, except for the constraints of hard-core exclusion.

(a) Calculate the entropy \(S\), as a function of the total energy \(E\). (Hint: \((V - a\omega)(V - (N - a)\omega) \approx (V - N\omega/2)^2\).)

(b) Calculate the equation of state of this gas.

(c) Show that the isothermal compressibility, \(\kappa_T = -V^{-1} ( \partial V/\partial P )_T\), is always positive.
solution:
(a) The microcanonical phase-space volume is
\[\Omega = \frac{1}{N!\, h^{3N}} \int_{\mathcal{H}=E} d^{3N}q\, d^{3N}p,\qquad E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{p_i^{2}}{2m}.
\]
Integrating over momenta as in the ideal gas and enforcing excluded volume by sequential placement yields
\[\int d^{3N}q = V (V-\omega) (V-2\omega) \cdots (V-(N-1)\omega) \approx \left( V - \frac{N\omega}{2} \right)^{N}.
\]
Thus
\[S \approx N k_B \ln\left[ \frac{e}{N} \left( V - \frac{N\omega}{2} \right) \left( \frac{4\pi m E e}{3 N h^{2}} \right)^{3/2} \right].\]

(b) Using \(P/T = (\partial S/\partial V)_{E,N}\) gives
\[\frac{P}{T} = \frac{N k_B}{V - N\omega/2} \;\Rightarrow\; P\left(V - \frac{N\omega}{2}\right) = N k_B T.\]

(c) From the equation of state at fixed \(T\):
\[\kappa_T = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_{T,N} = \frac{N k_B T}{P^{2} V} > 0,\]
so the isothermal compressibility is positive.
final_answers: ["S \\approx N k_B \\ln\\left[ \\dfrac{e}{N} \\left( V - \\dfrac{N\\omega}{2} \\right) \\left( \\dfrac{4\\pi m E e}{3 N h^{2}} \\right)^{3/2} \\right]", "P\\big(V - N\\omega/2\\big) = N k_B T", "\\kappa_T = N k_B T/(P^{2} V) > 0"]

question:
6. Glass: Liquid quartz, if cooled slowly, crystallizes at a temperature $T_m$, and releases latent heat $L$. Under more rapid cooling conditions, the liquid is supercooled and becomes glassy.

(a) As both phases of quartz are almost incompressible, there is no work input, and changes in internal energy satisfy $dE = T dS + \mu dN$. Use the extensivity condition to obtain the expression for $\mu$ in terms of $E$, $T$, $S$, and $N$.

(b) The heat capacity of crystalline quartz is approximately $C_X = \alpha T^3$, while that of glassy quartz is roughly $C_G = \beta T$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are constants. Assuming that the third law of thermodynamics applies to both crystalline and glass phases, calculate the entropies of the two phases at temperatures $T \le T_m$.

(c) At zero temperature the local bonding structure is similar in glass and crystalline quartz, so that they have approximately the same internal energy $E_0$. Calculate the internal energies of both phases at temperatures $T \le T_m$.

(d) Use the condition of thermal equilibrium between two phases to compute the equilibrium melting temperature $T_m$ in terms of $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

(e) Compute the latent heat $L$ in terms of $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

(f) Is the result in the previous part correct? If not, which of the steps leading to it is most likely to be incorrect?
solution:
(a) With only chemical work, write $dS = dE/T - (\mu/T) dN$. Entropy is extensive: $\lambda S = S(\lambda E, \lambda N)$. Differentiate with respect to $\lambda$ at $\lambda=1$ to obtain
$$
S = \left.\frac{\partial S}{\partial E}\right|_N E + \left.\frac{\partial S}{\partial N}\right|_E N = \frac{E}{T} - \frac{\mu N}{T} \implies \mu = \frac{E - T S}{N}.
$$

(b) Using $C=T\,(dS/dT)$ and $S(0)=0$:
$$
S_{\text{crystal}}(T) = \frac{N\alpha}{3} T^3,\qquad S_{\text{glass}}(T) = N\beta\,T.
$$

(c) With $dE = T dS$ at fixed $N$ and $B=0$, and $E(0)=E_0$:
$$
E_{\text{crystal}}(T) = E_0 + \frac{N\alpha}{4} T^4,\qquad E_{\text{glass}}(T) = E_0 + \frac{N\beta}{2} T^2.
$$

(d) Chemical equilibrium $\mu_{\text{crystal}}=\mu_{\text{glass}}$ with $\mu=(E - T S)/N$ gives
$$
\frac{\alpha}{12} T_m^4 = \frac{\beta}{2} T_m^2 \implies T_m = \sqrt{\frac{6\beta}{\alpha}}.
$$

(e) The latent heat for glass $\to$ crystal at $T_m$ is $L = T_m(S_{\text{crystal}} - S_{\text{glass}}) = N T_m\big(\tfrac{\alpha}{3} T_m^2 - \beta\big) = -N\beta T_m^2$ using part (d).

(f) The result $L<0$ is unphysical for crystallization (it would imply the crystal has higher entropy). The questionable assumption is that the glass obeys the third law with $S(0)=0$. Glass is non-ergodic and retains residual entropy as $T\to 0$, invalidating that step.
final_answers: ["\\mu = (E - T S)/N", "S_{\\text{crystal}} = (N\\alpha/3) T^3,\\; S_{\\text{glass}} = N\\beta T", "E_{\\text{crystal}} = E_0 + (N\\alpha/4) T^4,\\; E_{\\text{glass}} = E_0 + (N\\beta/2) T^2", "T_m = \\sqrt{6\\beta/\\alpha}", "L = -\\,N\\,\\beta\\,T_m^2", null]

question:
Evolution of entropy: The normalized ensemble density is a probability in the phase space $\Gamma$. This probability has an associated entropy $S(t) = -\int d\Gamma \, \rho(\Gamma, t) \ln \rho(\Gamma, t)$. 

(a) Show that if $\rho(\Gamma,t)$ satisfies Liouville's equation for a Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H}$, then $\frac{dS}{dt} = 0$.

(b) Using the method of Lagrange multipliers, find the function $\rho_{\max}(\Gamma)$ which maximizes the functional $S[\rho]$, subject to the constraint of fixed average energy, $\langle \mathcal{H} \rangle = \int d\Gamma \, \rho \, \mathcal{H} = E$.

(c) Show that the solution to part (b) is stationary, i.e. $\frac{\partial \rho_{\max}}{\partial t} = 0$.

(d) How can one reconcile the result in (a), with the observed increase in entropy as the system approaches the equilibrium density in (b)?
solution:
(a) With $S=-\int d\Gamma\, \rho\ln\rho$, $\dot S = -\int d\Gamma\, (\partial_t\rho)(\ln\rho+1)$. Liouville's equation gives $\partial_t\rho = -\{\rho,\mathcal H\} = -\sum_i(\partial_{q_i}\rho\,\partial_{p_i}\mathcal H - \partial_{p_i}\rho\,\partial_{q_i}\mathcal H)$. Integrating by parts in phase space and assuming vanishing boundary terms yields $\dot S=0$.

(b) Maximize $S[\rho]$ with constraints $\int d\Gamma\,\rho=1$ and $\int d\Gamma\,\rho\,\mathcal H=E$ via Lagrange multipliers $\alpha,\beta$: extremize $\int d\Gamma\, \rho\bigl[-\ln\rho - \alpha - \beta\mathcal H\bigr]$. Stationarity with respect to $\rho$ gives $-\ln\rho - \alpha - \beta\mathcal H - 1=0$, hence $\rho_{\max}(\Gamma)=C e^{-\beta\mathcal H(\Gamma)}$ with $C=e^{-(\alpha+1)}$ fixed by normalization.

(c) The stationary form satisfies $\partial_t\rho_{\max} = -\{\rho_{\max},\mathcal H\} = -C\{-e^{-\beta\mathcal H},\mathcal H\}=0$ since $\{\mathcal H,\mathcal H\}=0$.

(d) Fine-grained entropy is conserved under Liouville flow, but the distribution develops filaments. Any coarse-graining (finite resolution in $\Gamma$) loses information and yields an increasing coarse-grained entropy approaching the maximal value at $\rho_{\max}$.
final_answers: [0.0, "\\rho_{\\max}(\\Gamma)=C e^{-\\beta\\mathcal H(\\Gamma)}", 0.0, null]

question:
1. Continuous spins: In the standard O(n) model, n component unit vectors are placed on the sites of a lattice. The nearest neighbor spins are then connected by a bond J \vec{s}_i \cdot \vec{s}_j. In fact, if we are only interested in universal properties, any generalized interaction f(\vec{s}_i \cdot \vec{s}_j) leads to the same critical behavior. By analogy with the Ising model, a suitable choice is
$$
\exp \big[f (\vec{s}_i \cdot \vec{s}_j)\big] = 1 + (nt)\,\vec{s}_i \cdot \vec{s}_j,
$$
resulting in the so called loop model.

(a) Construct a high temperature expansion of the loop model (for the partition function Z) in the parameter t, on a two-dimensional hexagonal (honeycomb) lattice.

(b) Show that the limit n \to 0 describes the configurations of a single self-avoiding polymer on the lattice.
solution:
(a) The partition function for the loop model has the form
$$
Z = \int \{D s_i\} \prod_{\langle ij \rangle} \big[1 + (nt)\,\vec{s}_i \cdot \vec{s}_j\big],
$$
which we expand in powers of t. If the lattice has N_B nearest-neighbor bonds, the product generates 2^{N_B} terms. Each term corresponds to a graph in which a bond (i,j) is included if \(\vec{s}_i \cdot \vec{s}_j\) appears; each included bond carries a factor nt. As in the Ising model, integrals over \{\vec{s}_i\} leave only graphs with an even number of bonds incident on each site, since
$$
\int d s\, s_\alpha = \int d s\, s_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma = \dots = 0.
$$
On the honeycomb lattice, sites have coordination 3, so the only surviving graphs are those with two bonds at each site, i.e. collections of closed self-avoiding loops. Normalizing \(\int d s = 1\) and using
$$
\int d s\, s_\alpha s_\beta = \frac{\delta_{\alpha\beta}}{n}, \qquad \int d s'\, (s_\alpha s'_\alpha)(s'_\beta s''_\beta) = \frac{1}{n} s_\alpha s''_\alpha,
$$
propagates a single spin component around any loop and yields a factor n when the loop closes. Each loop of length \(\ell\) thus contributes \(n\,t^{\ell}\). Therefore
$$
Z = \sum_{\text{collections of self-avoiding loops}} n^{N_\ell}\, t^{N_b},
$$
where \(N_\ell\) is the number of loops and \(N_b\) the number of occupied bonds in the configuration.

(b) At exactly n=0, \(Z=1\). Taking the limit \(n\to 0\), the leading contribution, of order \(O(n^1)\), comes from configurations with a single loop: \(N_\ell=1\). The two-point function
$$
G_{\alpha\beta}(n-m) = \langle s_{n\alpha} s_{m\beta}\rangle = \frac{1}{Z} \int\{D s_i\}\, s_{n\alpha}s_{m\beta} \prod_{\langle ij \rangle} [1+ (nt) \vec{s}_i\cdot \vec{s}_j]
$$
reduces, in the \(n\to 0\) limit, to a sum over self-avoiding walks (SAWs) from m to n with weight \(t^{\ell}\) for a path of length \(\ell\), all spins along the path sharing the same component. Denoting by \(W_\ell(R)\) the number of SAWs of length \(\ell\) with end-to-end distance R, we have the generating relation
$$
\sum_{\ell} W_\ell(R)\, t^{\ell} = \lim_{n\to 0} G(R).
$$
Standard scaling then yields for the mean-square end-to-end distance
$$
